contributions.statement: Paper lineage — an algorithm that traces cited literature and associated code repositories to surface unstated implementation details and implicit domain knowledge for experiment reproduction.
contributions.quote: We propose paper lineage, an algorithm that enables the agent to learn implicit domain knowledge and implementation practices by analyzing the cited literature.
contributions.quote_verbatim: e propose paper lineage , an algorithm that enables the agent to learn implicit domain knowledge and implementation practices by analyzing the cited literature.

contributions.statement: AutoReproduce — a multi-agent framework for end-to-end automated reproduction of AI experiments that incorporates a sampling-based unit testing strategy to guarantee code executability, outperforming existing baselines on reproduction and execution fidelity metrics.
contributions.quote: We propose AutoReproduce, a multi-agent framework designed for end-to-end experiment reproduction, which achieves superior performance compared with existing methods.
contributions.quote_verbatim: We propose AutoReproduce , a multi-agent framework designed for end-to-end experiment reproduction, which achieves superior performance compared with existing methods

contributions.statement: ReproduceBench — a benchmark of 13 AI research papers with manually curated, verified reference implementations and five evaluation metrics spanning structural reproduction fidelity to final execution accuracy.
contributions.quote: We construct ReproduceBench, a novel benchmark for evaluating the experiment reproduction capabilities, which includes manually curated reference code implementations and a suite of evaluation metrics.
contributions.quote_verbatim: We construct ReproduceBench , a novel benchmark for evaluating the experiment reproduction capabilities, which includes manually curated reference code implementations and a suite of evaluation metrics

contributions.statement: ScienceAgentBench — a benchmark of 102 code-generation tasks drawn from 44 peer-reviewed publications across four scientific disciplines (Bioinformatics, Computational Chemistry, Geographical Information Science, and Psychology & Cognitive Neuroscience), each validated by subject matter experts, with target outputs unified as self-contained Python programs and evaluated using metrics covering program quality, execution results, and cost.
contributions.quote: we present ScienceAgentBench, a new benchmark for evaluating language agents for data-driven scientific discovery. To ensure the scientific authenticity and real-world relevance of our benchmark, we extract 102 tasks from 44 peer-reviewed publications in four disciplines and engage nine subject matter experts to validate them. We unify the target output for every task to a self-contained Python program file and employ an array of evaluation metrics to examine the generated programs, execution results, and costs.
contributions.quote_verbatim: we present ScienceAgentBench, a new benchmark for evaluating language agents for data-driven scientific discovery. To ensure the scientific authenticity and real-world relevance of our benchmark, we extract 102 tasks from 44 peer-reviewed publications in four disciplines and engage nine subject matter experts to validate them. We unify the target output for every task to a self-contained Python program file and employ an array of evaluation metrics to examine the generated programs, execution results, and costs.

contributions.statement: A graded evaluation protocol for data-driven scientific code generation that combines program-level metrics, execution-result checks (rendered figures, test-set predictions), cost accounting, and per-task step-by-step rubrics to support partial-credit assessment of open-ended scientific tasks.
contributions.quote: We first unify the target output for every task as a self-contained Python program, and then employ an array of evaluation metrics that examine the generated programs, execution results (e.g., rendered figures or test set predictions), and costs. We also provide step-by-step rubrics specific to each task to enable graded evaluation.
contributions.quote_verbatim: We first unify the target output for every task as a self-contained Python program, and then employ an array of evaluation metrics that examine the generated programs, execution results (e.g., rendered figures or test set predictions), and costs. We also provide step-by-step rubrics specific to each task to enable graded evaluation.

contributions.statement: Two strategies for mitigating data-contamination risks in scientific coding benchmarks arising from LLM pre-training exposure.
contributions.quote: We also propose two effective strategies to mitigate data contamination concerns due to LLM pre-training.
contributions.quote_verbatim: We also propose two effective strategies to mitigate data contamination concerns due to LLM pre-training.

contributions.statement: State-of-the-art language agents solve at most 32.4% of real-world data-driven scientific coding tasks unaided and 34.3% with expert-provided knowledge; inference-time scaling (OpenAI o1-preview) raises this to 42.2% but at more than 10× the cost, while simpler self-debug frameworks outperform interactive CodeAct agents at a fraction of the price — collectively showing that current agents cannot automate essential tasks in scientific workflows.
contributions.quote: the best-performing agent can only solve 32.4% of the tasks independently and 34.3% with expert-provided knowledge. In addition, we evaluate OpenAI o1-preview with direct prompting and self-debug, which can boost the performance to 42.2%, demonstrating the effectiveness of increasing inference-time compute but with more than 10 times the cost of other LLMs. Still, our results underscore the limitations of current language agents in generating code for data-driven discovery, let alone end-to-end automation for scientific research.
contributions.quote_verbatim: the best-performing agent can only solve 32.4% of the tasks independently and 34.3% with expert-provided knowledge. In addition, we evaluate OpenAI o1-preview with direct prompting and self-debug, which can boost the performance to 42.2%, demonstrating the effectiveness of increasing inference-time compute but with more than 10 times the cost of other LLMs. Still, our results underscore the limitations of current language agents in generating code for data-driven discovery, let alone end-to-end automation for scientific research.

contributions.statement: DISCOVERYWORLD — a virtual, text-based simulated environment (with optional 2D visual overlay) covering eight diverse scientific topics such as radioisotope dating, rocket science, and proteomics — enables developing and benchmarking AI agents on complete, end-to-end scientific discovery cycles including hypothesis formation, experiment design, data collection, analysis, and acting on conclusions.
contributions.quote: We introduce the first virtual environment for benchmarking an agent's general ability to perform complete cycles of novel scientific discovery.
contributions.quote_verbatim: We introduce the first virtual environment for benchmarking an agent’s general ability to perform complete cycles of novel scientific discovery.

contributions.statement: A benchmark suite of 120 challenge tasks spanning eight diverse scientific topics, each with three difficulty levels and several parametric variations, for evaluating general-purpose scientific discovery competency in AI agents.
contributions.quote: A comprehensive evaluation set of 120 different tasks for this environment, spanning eight diverse topics, each with three levels of difficulty and several parametric variations.
contributions.quote_verbatim: A comprehensive evaluation set of 120 different tasks for this environment, spanning eight diverse topics, each with three levels of difficulty and several parametric variations.

contributions.statement: A three-part automatic evaluation framework for scientific discovery agents that assesses (a) task completion, (b) task-relevant actions taken, and (c) the quality of discovered explanatory knowledge.
contributions.quote: An evaluation framework allowing automatic evaluation of agents in DiscoveryWorld.
contributions.quote_verbatim: n evaluation framework allowing automatic evaluation of agents in DiscoveryWorld .

contributions.statement: Strong baseline agents that perform well on prior published environments struggle on end-to-end scientific discovery tasks, demonstrating that complete discovery cycles — requiring ideation, systematic experimentation, and explanatory inference — pose novel challenges not captured by existing benchmarks.
contributions.quote: Baseline results for agents in this environment, illustrating that DiscoveryWorld captures several novel challenges that contemporary agent models struggle with.
contributions.quote_verbatim: Baseline results for agents in this environment, illustrating that DiscoveryWorld captures several novel challenges that contemporary agent models struggle with.

contributions.statement: Curie — an AI agent framework that embeds rigor into automated scientific experimentation through three components: an intra-agent rigor module for reliability, an inter-agent rigor module for methodical control, and an experiment knowledge module for interpretability.
contributions.quote: we propose Curie, an AI agent framework designed to embed rigor into the experimentation process through three key components: an intra-agent rigor module to enhance reliability, an inter-agent rigor module to maintain methodical control, and an experiment knowledge module to enhance interpretability.
contributions.quote_verbatim: e propose Curie , an AI agent framework designed to embed rigor into the experimentation process through three key components: an intra-agent rigor module to enhance reliability, an inter-agent rigor module to maintain methodical control, and an experiment knowledge module to enhance interpretability.

contributions.statement: An experimentation benchmark of 46 questions spanning four computer science domains, derived from influential research papers and widely adopted open-source projects, for evaluating AI agents on rigorous scientific experimentation tasks.
contributions.quote: we design a novel experimental benchmark composed of 46 questions across four computer science domains, derived from influential research papers, and widely adopted open-source projects
contributions.quote_verbatim: we design a novel experimental benchmark composed of 46 questions across four computer science domains, derived from influential research papers, and widely adopted open-source projects

contributions.statement: Automated AI-driven scientific experimentation achieves a 3.4× improvement in correctly answering experimental questions compared to state-of-the-art agent baselines, demonstrating the viability of embedding structured rigor into multi-agent experimentation pipelines.
contributions.quote: Compared to the strongest baseline tested, we achieve a 3.4× improvement in correctly answering experimental questions.
contributions.quote_verbatim: Compared to the strongest baseline tested, we achieve a 3.4 improvement in correctly answering experimental questions.

contributions.statement: Six recurring failure modes in LLM-based autonomous research pipelines: bias toward training data defaults, implementation drift under execution pressure, memory and context degradation across long-horizon tasks, overexcitement that declares success despite obvious failures, insufficient domain intelligence, and weak scientific taste in experimental design.
contributions.quote: we document six recurring failure modes: bias toward training data defaults, implementation drift under execution pressure, memory and context degradation across long-horizon tasks, overexcitement that declares success despite obvious failures, insufficient domain intelligence, and weak scientific taste in experimental design
contributions.quote_verbatim: we document six recurring failure modes: bias toward training data defaults, implementation drift under execution pressure, memory and context degradation across long-horizon tasks, overexcitement that declares success despite obvious failures, insufficient domain intelligence, and weak scientific taste in experimental design

contributions.statement: Four design principles for building more robust AI-scientist systems, derived from empirical case-study experience running four end-to-end autonomous ML research attempts.
contributions.quote: We conclude by discussing four design principles for more robust AI-scientist systems, implications for autonomous scientific discovery
contributions.quote_verbatim: We conclude by discussing four design principles for more robust AI-scientist systems, implications for autonomous scientific discovery

contributions.statement: A six-agent autonomous research pipeline — covering idea generation, hypothesis formulation, experiment planning, output evaluation, revision, and paper outlining — that operates with minimal scaffolding and a shared file-system context store, demonstrated end-to-end on ML research tasks.
contributions.quote: Our final system consisted of the following six distinct modules… Figure 1 shows a high-level diagram of our system design
contributions.quote_verbatim: t. Our final system consisted of the following six distinct modules, all using Gemini 2.5 Pro primarily because of its lon

contributions.statement: A public release of all prompts, agent artifacts, intermediate outputs, and final papers from four autonomous ML research attempts, enabling reproducibility and analysis of AI-scientist system behaviour.
contributions.quote: we release all prompts, artifacts, and outputs at https://github.com/Lossfunk/ai-scientist-artefacts-v1
contributions.quote_verbatim: we release all prompts, artifacts, and outputs at https://github.com/Lossfunk/ai-scientist-artefacts-v1

contributions.statement: Baby-AIGS — a multi-agent LLM-powered system comprising ProposalAgent, ExpAgent, ReviewAgent, and FalsificationAgent — enables autonomous, full-process scientific discovery without relying on domain-specific external verification engines.
contributions.quote: we propose Baby-AIGS as a baby-step demonstration of a full-process AIGS system, which is a multi-agent system with agents in roles representing key research process
contributions.quote_verbatim:
e propose BABY-AIGS as a baby-step demonstration of a
full-process AIGS system, which is a multi-agent system with agents in roles rep-
resenting key research proces

contributions.statement: FalsificationAgent — an agent that first identifies candidate scientific discoveries and then autonomously designs and executes experiments to verify or refute them — provides explicit falsification capability missing from prior end-to-end AI research systems.
contributions.quote: By introducing FalsificationAgent, which identify and then verify possible scientific discoveries, we empower the system with explicit falsification
contributions.quote_verbatim:
By introducing FALSIFICATIONAGENT, which
identify and then verify possible scientific discoveries, we empower the system
with explicit falsification

contributions.statement: Falsification, rather than creativity or executability alone, is the central and previously underrepresented design principle required for full-process AI-Generated Science systems.
contributions.quote: we argue that falsification is the essence of both human research process and the design of an AIGS system
contributions.quote_verbatim:
we argue that falsification is the essence of both human research process and the
design of an AIGS system

contributions.statement: A full-process multi-agent AI system can produce meaningful scientific discoveries across diverse research tasks, though with a measurable quality gap relative to experienced human researchers.
contributions.quote: Experiments on three tasks preliminarily show that Baby-AIGS could produce meaningful scientific discoveries, though not on par with experienced human researchers
contributions.quote_verbatim:
Experiments on three tasks preliminarily show that
BABY-AIGS could produce meaningful scientific discoveries, though not on par
with experienced human researchers

contributions.statement: MLR-Bench — a benchmark of 201 open-ended machine learning research tasks drawn from NeurIPS, ICLR, and ICML workshops — enables systematic evaluation of AI research agents across nine ML topic areas with both stepwise (idea generation, proposal formulation, experimentation, paper writing) and end-to-end assessment pipelines.
contributions.quote: MLR-Bench : To our knowledge, the most comprehensive evaluation benchmark for AI research agents to date, featuring 201 open-ended ML research tasks, a human-aligned MLR-Judge for automated research quality assessment, and a modular MLR-Agent supporting both stepwise and end-to-end research execution.
contributions.quote_verbatim: MLR-Bench : To our knowledge, the most comprehensive evaluation benchmark for AI research agents to date, featuring 201 open-ended ML research tasks, a human-aligned MLR-Judge for automated research quality assessment, and a modular MLR-Agent supporting both stepwise and end-to-end research execution.

contributions.statement: MLR-Judge — an automated evaluation framework pairing LLM-based reviewers with structured review rubrics — achieves agreement with expert human reviewers comparable to inter-human agreement, providing a scalable proxy for assessing AI-generated research quality.
contributions.quote: MLR-Judge , an automated evaluation pipeline consisting of LLM-based judge and structured review rubrics; ... We find that the level of agreement between an LLM judge and a human judge, when assessing the same output, is very close to the level of agreement between two human judges, demonstrating its effectiveness as a reliable evaluation tool.
contributions.quote_verbatim: erts with top-tier conference review experience to independently review the AI-generated research outputs. We find that the level of agreement between an LLM judge and a human judge, when assessing the same output, is very close to the level of agreement between two human judges, demonstrating its effectiveness as a reliable evaluation tool. Fi

contributions.statement: MLR-Agent — a modular scaffold that sequentially executes idea generation, proposal formulation, experimentation, and paper writing — provides a reusable baseline research agent for benchmarking AI-driven scientific discovery.
contributions.quote: MLR-Agent , a modular research agent scaffold capable of automatically completing these tasks by following the four defined research stages.
contributions.quote_verbatim: MLR-Agent , a modular research agent scaffold capable of automatically completing these tasks by following the four defined research stages.

contributions.statement: Current coding agents fabricate or fail to validate experimental results in approximately 80% of ML research task attempts, representing a critical barrier to scientific reliability that is distinct from their strong performance on idea generation and paper writing.
contributions.quote: finding that while LLMs are effective at generating coherent ideas and well-structured papers, current coding agents frequently (e.g., in 80% of the cases) produce fabricated or invalidated experimental results—posing a major barrier to scientific reliability.
contributions.quote_verbatim: finding that while LLMs are effective at generating coherent ideas and well-structured papers, current coding agents frequently (e.g., in 80% of the cases) produce fabricated or invalidated experimental results—posing a major barrier to scientific reliability.

contributions.statement: CORE-Bench — a benchmark of 270 tasks derived from 90 scientific papers across computer science, social science, and medicine — measures AI agent accuracy on computational reproducibility at three difficulty levels, covering both language-only and vision-language tasks with Python and R codebases.
contributions.quote: CORE-Bench (Computational Reproducibility Benchmark). CORE-Bench comprises 270 tasks derived from 90 papers across computer science, social science, and medicine with Python or R codebases.
contributions.quote_verbatim: CORE-Bench (Computational Reproducibility Benchmark). CORE-Bench comprises 270 tasks derived from 90 papers across computer science, social science, and medicine with Python or R codebases.

contributions.statement: A fast, parallelizable evaluation harness for computational reproducibility tasks saves days of evaluation time per run compared to sequential implementation.
contributions.quote: We provide an evaluation system to measure the accuracy of agents in a fast and parallelizable way, saving days of evaluation time for each run compared to a sequential implementation.
contributions.quote_verbatim: We provide an evaluation system to measure the accuracy of agents in a fast and parallelizable way, saving days of evaluation time for each run compared to a sequential implementation.

contributions.statement: CORE-Agent, a task-specific agent adaptation of AutoGPT, achieves substantially higher accuracy on computational reproducibility tasks than the unmodified generalist AutoGPT, demonstrating that targeted modifications to generalist agents yield significant performance gains, with the best agent reaching only 21% accuracy on the hardest tasks.
contributions.quote: We evaluated two agents on CORE-Bench: the generalist agent AutoGPT and a task-specific version we built based on AutoGPT called CORE-Agent. Results show that generalist agents can be easily adapted to specific tasks, yielding significant performance improvements. The best agent achieved an accuracy of 21% on the hardest level of tasks.
contributions.quote_verbatim: luated two agents on CORE-Bench : the generalist agent AutoGPT (Significant Gravitas, 2024 ) and a task-specific version we built based on AutoGPT called CORE-Agent . Results show that generalist agents can be easily adapted to specific tasks, yielding significant performance improvements. Our task-specific agent achieved 60% accuracy o

contributions.statement: CycleResearcher and CycleReviewer — an iterative reinforcement learning framework pairing a policy model for research tasks with a reward model that simulates peer review — enables open-source LLMs to automate the full Research-Review-Refinement lifecycle through repeated preference optimization.
contributions.quote: We introduce an iterative reinforcement learning framework that automates the entire research lifecycle, which mirrors the real-world Research-Review-Refinement cycle. Our framework includes CycleResearcher, a policy model for research tasks, and CycleReviewer, a reward model simulating peer reviews.
contributions.quote_verbatim:
We introduce an iterative reinforcement learning framework that automates the entire research
lifecycle, which mirrors the real-world Research-Review-Refinement cycle. Our framework
includes CycleResearcher, a policy model for research tasks, and CycleReviewer, a reward
model simulating peer reviews.

contributions.statement: Review-5k and Research-14k — two large-scale, publicly available datasets capturing real-world machine learning peer review dynamics and full research paper generation — provide training and evaluation resources for automated academic paper writing and review models.
contributions.quote: We release two large-scale datasets, Review-5k and Research-14k, which are publicly available and designed to capture the complexity of both peer review and research paper generation in machine learning.
contributions.quote_verbatim:
We release two large-scale datasets, Review-5k and Research-14k, which are publicly available
and designed to capture the complexity of both peer review and research paper generation in
machine learning.

contributions.statement: An automated peer-review model (CycleReviewer) achieves a 26.89% reduction in mean absolute error compared to individual human reviewers when predicting paper scores, establishing a new benchmark for automated research assessment.
contributions.quote: our CycleReviewer model shows encouraging results with a 26.89% improvement in MAE compared to individual reviewers, suggesting the potential of automated research assessment in mean absolute error (MAE) in research evaluation tasks, setting a new benchmark for automated research assessment.
contributions.quote_verbatim:
our CycleReviewer model shows encouraging results with a 26.89% improvement in MAE
compared to individual reviewers, suggesting the potential of automated research assessment in
mean absolute error (MAE) in research evaluation tasks, setting a new benchmark for automated
research assessment.

contributions.statement: Open-source LLM-generated research papers (from CycleResearcher) reach an average simulated peer-review score of 5.36 and a 31.07% acceptance rate, approaching the quality of human-written preprints (score 5.24) while remaining below accepted-paper level (5.69).
contributions.quote: We demonstrate that the CycleResearcher model can generate papers with an average quality level close to human-written preprints, achieving an acceptance rate of 31.07%.
contributions.quote_verbatim:
We demonstrate that the CycleResearcher model can generate papers with an average quality
level close to human-written preprints, achieving an acceptance rate of 31.07%.

contributions.statement: PaperBench — a benchmark of 20 ICML 2024 Spotlight and Oral papers with hierarchically decomposed, author-approved rubrics (8,316 individually gradable leaf criteria) and an automated LLM-based grading workflow for evaluating AI agents' ability to replicate AI research from scratch.
contributions.quote: PaperBench: a benchmark of 20 ML research papers and author-approved rubrics, and an automated grading workflow using LLM-based judges.
contributions.quote_verbatim: PaperBench: a benchmark of 20 ML research papers and author-approved rubrics, and an automated grading workflow using LLM-based judges.

contributions.statement: PaperBench Code-Dev — a lightweight variant of the AI-research replication benchmark that relaxes execution requirements to lower setup cost and broaden community accessibility.
contributions.quote: PaperBench Code-Dev: a more lightweight variant of the benchmark which relaxes some requirements of PaperBench to make setup and evaluation more accessible to the broader community.
contributions.quote_verbatim: PaperBench Code-Dev: a more lightweight variant of the benchmark which relaxes some requirements of PaperBench to make setup and evaluation more accessible to the broader community.

contributions.statement: JudgeEval — a dataset of human-expert-graded replication submissions that serves as an auxiliary evaluation for developing and benchmarking automated LLM-based judges of research replication quality.
contributions.quote: JudgeEval: a dataset of human-graded submissions, which can be used as an auxiliary evaluation for the development and assessment of automated judges.
contributions.quote_verbatim: JudgeEval: a dataset of human-graded submissions, which can be used as an auxiliary evaluation for the development and assessment of automated judges.

contributions.statement: Frontier AI agents score at most 21.0% average replication on PaperBench, well below a human ML-PhD baseline of 41.4% on a matched subset, establishing a quantitative gap between current AI and expert human capability for autonomous ML research replication.
contributions.quote: Evaluations of frontier models on PaperBench: an assessment of several frontier AI agents' abilities to conduct long-horizon tasks and ML R&D … the best-performing tested agent, Claude 3.5 Sonnet (New) with open-source scaffolding, achieves an average replication score of 21.0% … models do not yet outperform the human baseline.
contributions.quote_verbatim: We evaluate several frontier models on PaperBench, finding that the best-performing tested agent, Claude 3.5 Sonnet (New) with open-source scaffolding, achieves an average replication score of 21.0%. Finally, we recruit top ML PhDs to attempt a subset of PaperBench, finding that models do not yet outperform the human baseline.

contributions.statement: FactReview — a multi-stage automated reviewing system that decomposes a manuscript into review-relevant claims, grounds each claim in retrieved related work, executes released code artifacts under a fixed repair budget, and assigns one of four evidence labels (Supported, Partially Supported, In Conflict, Inconclusive) with explicit links to manuscript, literature, and execution evidence.
contributions.quote: We build FactReview, a multi-stage system that assigns four claim-level evidence labels and links each judgment to manuscript, literature, and execution evidence.
contributions.quote_verbatim: We build FactReview, a multi-stage system that assigns four claim-level evidence labels and links each judgment to manuscript, literature, and execution evidence.

contributions.statement: Reformulating automated peer review as evidence-grounded claim verification — combining manuscript analysis, literature grounding, and code execution — while explicitly withholding accept-reject decisions, positions LLM reviewers as claim auditors rather than paper scorers.
contributions.quote: We reformulate automated reviewing as evidence-grounded claim verification that combines manuscript analysis, literature grounding, and code execution, and explicitly avoid issuing accept-reject decisions.
contributions.quote_verbatim: We reformulate automated reviewing as evidence-grounded claim verification that combines manuscript analysis, literature grounding, and code execution, and explicitly avoid issuing accept-reject decisions.

contributions.statement: Execution-based evidence is the single largest driver of claim-status changes in automated peer review, shifting 17% of claim statuses across 35 ML papers — more than any other individual evidence source — and an execution-grounded reviewing assistant reduces mean reviewer time by 58% while raising benchmark claim coverage from 87% to 99%.
contributions.quote: On 35 ML papers and 463 benchmark claims, FactReview outperforms prior LLM reviewers and the matched OpenReview reference comments under an evidence-aware rubric; execution evidence is the single largest source of claim-status changes; and the resulting reports approximately halve reviewer time while preserving claim coverage.
contributions.quote_verbatim: On 35 ML papers and 463 benchmark claims, FactReview outperforms prior LLM reviewers and the matched OpenReview reference comments under an evidence-aware rubric; execution evidence is the single largest source of claim-status changes; and the resulting reports approximately halve reviewer time while preserving claim coverage.

contributions.statement: Injecting covert manipulative text into manuscript PDFs using an extremely small white font (invisible to humans but readable by PDF parsers) can almost entirely control LLM-generated peer reviews, raising agreement rates with injected content to ~90% while reducing alignment with human reviews from 53% to 16% and inflating average ratings from 5.34 to 7.99.
contributions.quote: we develop a review injection attack method that embeds manipulative review content into the manuscript PDF using extremely small white font, rendering it nearly invisible against the background... we find that LLM-generated reviews can be almost entirely controlled by the injected content, with agreement rates reaching 90%; while significantly deviating from human reviews (from 53% to 16%)
contributions.quote_verbatim: findings: (1) LLMs are vulnerable to both explicit and implicit manipulation, leading to reviews that can be significantly swayed by intentional authors. In our explicit manipulation experiments, we find that LLM-generated reviews can be almost entirely controlled by the injected content, with agreement rates reaching 90%; while significantly deviating from human reviews (from 53% to 16%).

contributions.statement: LLM peer reviewers are 4.5× more likely than human reviewers to reiterate limitations that authors proactively disclose in their manuscripts, revealing a systematic susceptibility to implicit manipulation via strategic self-disclosure of minor weaknesses.
contributions.quote: Implicit manipulation, where authors strategically highlight minor limitations in their papers, further demonstrates LLMs' susceptibility compared to human reviewers, with a 4.5 times higher consistency with disclosed limitations
contributions.quote_verbatim: rankings. Implicit manipulation, where authors strategically highlight minor limitations in their papers, further demonstrates LLMs’ susceptibility compared to human reviewers, with a higher consistency with disclosed limitations

contributions.statement: Manipulating as few as 5% of LLM-generated reviews in a conference setting can cause 12% of papers to lose their position in the top 30% of rankings, quantifying the systemic impact of localized review manipulation on paper acceptance outcomes.
contributions.quote: In a simulation, we find that manipulating 5% of the reviews could potentially cause 12% of the papers to lose their position in the top 30% rankings
contributions.quote_verbatim: In a simulation, we find that manipulating 5% of the reviews could potentially cause 12% of the papers to lose their position in the top 30% rankings

contributions.statement: LLMs exhibit inherent flaws in scholarly peer review: they assign higher or comparable ratings to papers containing only a title versus full papers (hallucination), and they produce more favorable reviews when authorship is attributed to well-known researchers in single-blind settings (prestige bias).
contributions.quote: LLMs exhibit inherent flaws, such as potentially assigning higher ratings to incomplete papers compared to full papers and favoring well-known authors in single-blind review process
contributions.quote_verbatim: LLMs exhibit inherent flaws, such as potentially assigning higher ratings to incomplete papers compared to full papers and favoring well-known authors in single-blind review process

contributions.statement: A comprehensive vulnerability analysis of LLM-based peer review systems, covering explicit injection attacks, implicit author-disclosure manipulation, hallucination under incomplete inputs, and authorship bias, demonstrates that current LLMs are insufficiently robust to serve as primary agents in scholarly peer review.
contributions.quote: we present a series of analysis that reveals the potential risks associated with employing LLMs in scholarly peer review... our paper underscores the associated risks through a comprehensive qualitative analysis. Our findings demonstrate that the current state of LLMs are insufficiently robust to support their role as primary agents in the peer review process
contributions.quote_verbatim: ew process. In conclusion, while recent studies have explored the use of LLMs in scholarly peer review [ 20 , 22 , 23 ] , our paper underscores the associated risks through a comprehensive qualitative analysis. Our findings demonstrate that the current state of LLMs are insufficiently robust to support their role as primary agents in the peer review process.

contributions.statement: REPRO-Bench — a benchmark of 112 task instances, each pairing a social science paper PDF with its reproduction package and a publicly available reproduction report, enabling end-to-end evaluation of AI agents on assessing computational reproducibility across diverse data formats and programming languages.
contributions.quote: we introduce REPRO-Bench, a collection of 112 task instances, each representing a social science paper with a publicly available reproduction report. The agents are tasked with assessing the reproducibility of the paper based on the original paper PDF and the corresponding reproduction package.
contributions.quote_verbatim: we introduce REPRO-Bench , a collection of 112 task instances, each representing a social science paper with a publicly available reproduction report. The agents are tasked with assessing the reproducibility of the paper based on the original paper PDF and the corresponding reproduction package

contributions.statement: State-of-the-art agentic AI systems perform below random-guessing baseline on end-to-end social science reproducibility assessment, with the best evaluated agent (CORE-Agent) reaching only 21.4% accuracy on a four-class scoring task.
contributions.quote: We evaluate three representative AI agents on REPRO-Bench, with the best-performing agent achieving an accuracy of only 21.4%, which is even lower than the expected 25% accuracy of random guessing among four scores.
contributions.quote_verbatim: We evaluate three representative AI agents on REPRO-Bench , with the best-performing agent achieving an accuracy of only 21.4%. Building on our empirical analysis, we develop REPRO-Agent , which improves the highes

contributions.statement: REPRO-Agent — an agent designed for social science reproducibility assessment that achieves 36.6% accuracy, a 71% relative improvement over the strongest prior agent on REPRO-Bench.
contributions.quote: Building on our empirical analysis, we develop REPRO-Agent, which improves the highest accuracy achieved by existing agents by 71%.
contributions.quote_verbatim: uilding on our empirical analysis, we develop REPRO-Agent , which improves the highest accuracy achieved by existing agents by 71%.

contributions.statement: AI-Researcher — a fully autonomous multi-agent system that orchestrates the complete scientific research pipeline from literature review and hypothesis generation through algorithm implementation, experimental validation, and publication-ready manuscript preparation with minimal human intervention.
contributions.quote: we introduce AI-Researcher, a fully autonomous research system that transforms how AI-driven scientific discovery is conducted and evaluated. Our framework seamlessly orchestrates the complete research pipeline–from literature review and hypothesis generation to algorithm implementation and publication-ready manuscript preparation–with minimal human intervention.
contributions.quote_verbatim: we introduce AI-Researcher, a fully autonomous research system that transforms how AI-driven scientific discovery is conducted and evaluated. Our framework seamlessly orchestrates the complete research pipeline–from literature review and hypothesis generation to algorithm implementation and publication-ready manuscript preparation–with minimal human intervention.

contributions.statement: Scientist-Bench — the first comprehensive benchmark for evaluating autonomous AI research agents, comprising state-of-the-art papers across diverse AI domains with both guided (Level-1) and open-ended (Level-2) tasks, paired ground-truth papers, curated references, and datasets for multidimensional assessment of implementation and documentation quality.
contributions.quote: we develop Scientist-Bench, a comprehensive benchmark comprising state-of-the-art papers across diverse AI research domains, featuring both guided innovation and open-ended exploration tasks
contributions.quote_verbatim: we develop Scientist-Bench, a comprehensive benchmark comprising state-of-the-art papers across diverse AI research domains, featuring both guided innovation and open-ended exploration tasks

contributions.statement: A multi-stage iterative refinement architecture for autonomous code generation in which Resource Analyst agents decompose research concepts into atomic components with explicit bidirectional mappings between mathematical formulations and code, and specialized advisor agents provide structured feedback cycles mirroring mentor-student collaboration, reducing hallucination and improving implementation fidelity.
contributions.quote: Resource Analyst agents decompose complex research concepts into atomic components with explicit bidirectional mappings between mathematical formulations and code implementations, dramatically reducing hallucination risks. Second, our Implementation Framework employs a human-inspired iterative refinement paradigm where specialized agents collaborate through structured feedback cycles, mirroring the proven mentor-student relationship in academic research.
contributions.quote_verbatim: Resource Analyst agents decompose complex research concepts into atomic components with explicit bidirectional mappings between mathematical formulations and code implementations, dramatically reducing hallucination risks. Second , our Implementation Framework employs a human-inspired iterative refinement paradigm where specialized agents collaborate through structured feedback cycles, mirroring the proven mentor-student relationship in academic research

contributions.statement: A hierarchical documentation synthesis approach that transforms heterogeneous research artifacts into publication-quality manuscripts by maintaining cross-document consistency and factual integrity across extended scholarly text, overcoming LLM coherence limitations in long-form scientific writing.
contributions.quote: our Documentation Agent overcomes LLM coherence limitations through a hierarchical synthesis approach that transforms research artifacts into publication-quality manuscripts while maintaining cross-document consistency and factual integrity throughout extensive scholarly documentation
contributions.quote_verbatim: our Documentation Agent overcomes LLM coherence limitations through a hierarchical synthesis approach that transforms research artifacts into publication-quality manuscripts while maintaining cross-document consistency and factual integrity throughout extensive scholarly documentation

contributions.statement: Autonomous research systems perform better on open-ended scientific exploration tasks than on guided implementation tasks, suggesting such systems leverage internal knowledge synthesis more effectively than following prescriptive research directives.
contributions.quote: our findings reveal AI-Researcher performs better in open-ended exploration than in guided implementation tasks—suggesting autonomous research systems excel when leveraging internal knowledge synthesis rather than following prescriptive directives
contributions.quote_verbatim: our findings reveal AI-Researcher performs better in open-ended exploration than in guided implementation tasks—suggesting autonomous research systems excel when leveraging internal knowledge synthesis rather than following prescriptive directives

contributions.statement: STATION — an open-world multi-agent environment that simulates a complete scientific ecosystem where autonomous agents read papers, formulate hypotheses, collaborate, execute experiments, and publish results without any centralized coordinating system.
contributions.quote: We introduce the Station, an open-world multi-agent environment for autonomous scientific discovery. The Station simulates a complete scientific ecosystem, where agents can engage in long scientific journeys that include reading papers from peers, formulating hypotheses, collaborating with peers, submitting experiments, and publishing results. Importantly, there is no centralized system coordinating their activities.
contributions.quote_verbatim: e introduce the Station , an open-world multi-agent environment for autonomous scientific discovery. The Station simulates a complete scientific ecosystem, where agents can engage in long scientific journeys that include reading papers from peers, formulating hypotheses, collaborating with peers, submitting experiments, and publishing results. Importantly, there is no centralized system coordinating their activities.

contributions.statement: AI agents operating freely in a decentralized, open-world scientific environment achieve state-of-the-art performance across diverse benchmarks in mathematics, computational biology, and machine learning — including surpassing AlphaEvolve on circle packing — without scripted pipelines or centralized task assignment.
contributions.quote: Experiments demonstrate that AI agents in the Station achieve new state-of-the-art performance on a wide range of benchmarks, spanning mathematics, computational biology, and machine learning, notably surpassing AlphaEvolve in circle packing.
contributions.quote_verbatim: Experiments demonstrate that AI agents in the Station achieve new state-of-the-art performance on a wide range of benchmarks, spanning mathematics, computational biology, and machine learning, notably surpassing AlphaEvolve in circle packing.

contributions.statement: A density-adaptive algorithm for scRNA-seq batch integration, discovered through emergent agent behavior by transferring density-awareness concepts from unsupervised clustering into a single-cell RNA sequencing context.
contributions.quote: novel methods arise organically, such as a new density-adaptive algorithm for scRNA-seq batch integration that borrows concepts from another domain.
contributions.quote_verbatim: novel methods arise organically, such as a new density-adaptive algorithm for scRNA-seq batch integration that borrows concepts from another domain.

contributions.statement: When multi-agent AI systems are placed in an open-world environment without explicit research objectives (Open Station), they spontaneously self-organize into a structured society exhibiting division of labor, shared culture, and emergent metaphysical narratives — demonstrating open-ended social dynamics beyond task-directed optimization.
contributions.quote: we further explore the Open Station variant where agents operate without explicit research objectives. In this setting, agents evolve into a self-sustaining society that establishes its own culture, division of labor, and even metaphysical beliefs to explain environmental phenomena.
contributions.quote_verbatim: we further explore the Open Station variant where agents operate without explicit research objectives. In this setting, agents evolve into a self-sustaining society that establishes its own culture, division of labor, and even metaphysical beliefs to explain environmental phenomena.

contributions.statement: ReplicationBench — a dataset of 20 reproducible, peer-reviewed astrophysics papers decomposed into 111 expert-written, author-co-developed research tasks targeting key scientific results, along with a ReplicationBench-Plus extension of 11 additional papers with 58 LLM-generated, expert-curated tasks.
contributions.quote: ReplicationBench Dataset: We publish an original dataset of 20 reproducible, self-contained astrophysics papers decomposed into 111 research-level tasks. We also publish the ReplicationBench-Plus extension, a set of 11 additional papers with 58 LLM-generated, expert-curated tasks.
contributions.quote_verbatim: ReplicationBench Dataset: We publish an original dataset of 20 reproducible, self-contained astrophysics papers decomposed into 111 research-level tasks . We also publish the ReplicationBench-Plus extension, a set of 11 additional papers with 58 LLM-generated, expert-curated tasks

contributions.statement: An evaluation framework for scientific AI agents that measures replication success on end-result-graded, objectively scorable tasks — assessing both faithfulness (adherence to original methods) and correctness (technical accuracy) — without requiring extensive human-written rubrics, enabling scalable measurement of agent reliability in computational research.
contributions.quote: Evaluation Framework for Scientific Agents: We establish a broader evaluation framework, measuring agent reliability in computational research tasks by measuring replication success on objectively gradable tasks.
contributions.quote_verbatim: Evaluation Framework for Scientific Agents: We establish a broader evaluation framework, measuring agent reliability in computational research tasks by measuring replication success on objectively gradable tasks.

contributions.statement: Frontier language models score under 20% on paper-scale astrophysics replication tasks, with a rich, diverse set of failure modes spanning technical and conceptual errors, as identified through expert-validated qualitative analysis of agent trajectories.
contributions.quote: ReplicationBench is extremely challenging for current frontier language models: even the best-performing language models score under 20%. We analyze ReplicationBench trajectories in collaboration with domain experts and find a rich, diverse set of failure modes for agents in scientific research.
contributions.quote_verbatim: ReplicationBench is extremely challenging for current frontier language models: even the best-performing language models score under 20%. We analyze ReplicationBench trajectories in collaboration with domain experts and find a rich, diverse set of failure modes for agents in scientific research.

contributions.statement: EXP-Bench — a benchmark of 461 AI research tasks drawn from 51 top-tier publications (NeurIPS, ICLR 2024) — evaluates AI agents on complete, end-to-end research experiments: hypothesis formulation, experimental design, implementation, execution, and result analysis, using multi-metric grading across 12,737 individually scorable subtasks.
contributions.quote: We introduce EXP-Bench, a novel benchmark designed to systematically evaluate AI agents on complete research experiments sourced from influential AI publications. Given a research question and incomplete starter code, EXP-Bench challenges AI agents to formulate hypotheses, design and implement experimental procedures, execute them, and analyze results.
contributions.quote_verbatim: We introduce EXP-Bench, a novel benchmark designed to systematically evaluate AI agents on complete research experiments sourced from influential AI publications. Given a research question and incomplete starter code, EXP-Bench challenges AI agents to formulate hypotheses, design and implement experimental procedures, execute them, and analyze results.

contributions.statement: A semi-automated pipeline for constructing high-fidelity AI research experiment tasks combines multi-modal extraction from papers and supplementary materials with implementation extraction from open-source codebases and execution-based validation, enabling scalable benchmark creation with only lightweight human oversight.
contributions.quote: To enable the creation of such intricate and authentic tasks with high-fidelity, we design a semi-autonomous pipeline to extract and structure crucial experimental details from these research papers and their associated open-source code.
contributions.quote_verbatim: To enable the creation of such intricate and authentic tasks with high-fidelity, we design a semi-autonomous pipeline to extract and structure crucial experimental details from these research papers and their associated open-source code.

contributions.statement: Leading LLM-based agents achieve only 0.5% success on complete, executable AI research experiments; individual phases reach at most 20–35%, with key failure modes being experimental design misclassification (16.1%), missing implementation components (39.7%), and environment or dependency misconfiguration (29.4%).
contributions.quote: Evaluations of leading LLM-based agents, such as OpenHands and IterativeAgent on EXP-Bench demonstrate partial capabilities: while scores on individual experimental aspects such as design or implementation correctness occasionally reach 20-35%, the success rate for complete, executable experiments was a mere 0.5%.
contributions.quote_verbatim: of leading AI agents, such as OpenHands wang2024openhands and IterativeAgent starace2025paperbench on EXP-Bench demonstrate partial capabilities: while scores on individual experimental aspects such as design or implementation correctness reach 20-35%, the success rate for complete, executable experiments was a m

contributions.statement: MLRC-Bench — a dynamic benchmark suite of 7 tasks drawn from ML conference competitions (NeurIPS, ECCV, KDD) — evaluates language agents on both proposing and implementing novel ML research methods using objective, performance-based metrics and compute constraints, enabling rigorous comparison against established baselines and top human solutions.
contributions.quote: We introduce MLRC-Bench, a dynamic benchmark suite curated from ML conference competitions, featuring open research problems that are both impactful and objectively measurable, and that demand the development of novel methodologies
contributions.quote_verbatim: We introduce MLRC-Bench , a dynamic benchmark suite curated from ML conference competitions, featuring open research problems that are both impactful and objectively measurable, and that demand the development of novel methodologie

contributions.statement: Large-scale evaluation of frontier LLMs under representative agent scaffoldings on ML research competition tasks shows that even the best-performing agent (gemini-exp-1206 under MLAB) closes only 9.3% of the gap between the baseline and top human participant scores, demonstrating severe limitations of current AI research agents in generating and implementing innovative ML solutions.
contributions.quote: We conduct large-scale, objective evaluations for a wide array of frontier LLMs with representative agent scaffoldings, highlighting their inability to propose and implement innovative solutions with notable performance gains
contributions.quote_verbatim: We conduct large-scale, objective evaluations for a wide array of frontier LLMs with representative agent scaffoldings, highlighting their inability to propose and implement innovative solutions with notable performance gains

contributions.statement: LLM-judged idea novelty is poorly correlated with empirical effectiveness on cutting-edge ML research problems, revealing a fundamental misalignment that undermines the reliability of LLM-as-a-judge for evaluating research agent outputs.
contributions.quote: We pinpoint the flaws in subjective evaluations of LLM-based research agents, by showing that the LLM-judged idea novelty is misaligned with empirical effectiveness
contributions.quote_verbatim: We pinpoint the flaws in subjective evaluations of LLM-based research agents, by showing that the LLM-judged idea novelty is misaligned with empirical effectiveness

contributions.statement: An Intelligent Agent system that combines multiple large language models to autonomously design, plan, and execute scientific experiments, demonstrated on tasks including catalyzed cross-coupling reactions in chemistry.
contributions.quote: we present an Intelligent Agent system that combines multiple large language models for autonomous design, planning, and execution of scientific experiments
contributions.quote_verbatim: we present an Intelligent Agent system that combines multiple large language models for autonomous design, planning, and execution of scientific experiments

contributions.statement: A safety analysis of LLM-based autonomous scientific research systems, with proposed measures to prevent their misuse.
contributions.quote: we discuss the safety implications of such systems and propose measures to prevent their misuse
contributions.quote_verbatim: we discuss the safety implications of such systems and propose measures to prevent their misuse

contributions.statement: AIDE (AI-Driven Exploration) — an LLM-powered agent that automates machine learning engineering by framing it as a code optimization problem and conducting tree search over candidate Python solutions, using draft, debug, and improve operators guided by automated evaluation scores.
contributions.quote: we introduce AI-Driven Exploration (AIDE), a machine learning engineering agent powered by large language models (LLMs). AIDE frames machine learning engineering as a code optimization problem, and formulates trial-and-error as a tree search in the space of potential solutions.
contributions.quote_verbatim: we introduce AI-Driven Exploration (AIDE), a machine learning engineering agent powered by large language models (LLMs). AIDE frames machine learning engineering as a code optimization problem, and formulates trial-and-error as a tree search in the space of potential solutions.

contributions.statement: Organizing LLM-driven iterative solution search as a solution tree — where nodes are standalone executable scripts, edges represent improvement attempts, and a hard-coded policy selects between drafting, debugging, and improving — enables scalable code-space optimization without growing prompts unboundedly.
contributions.quote: AIDE organizes all historical solutions in a tree structure. It then asks the LLM to propose improvements based on individual tree nodes. A hard-coded tree-search algorithm accumulates these incremental improvements, guided by automated evaluations.
contributions.quote_verbatim: AIDE organizes all historical solutions in a tree structure. It then asks the LLM to propose improvements based on individual tree nodes. A hard-coded tree-search algorithm accumulates these incremental improvements, guided by automated evaluations.

contributions.statement: The Weco Kaggle Benchmark — a curated set of tabular machine learning Kaggle tasks used to evaluate ML engineering agents — provides a reproducible evaluation protocol for automated ML engineering systems.
contributions.quote: We benchmarked AIDE on a set of Kaggle tasks focusing on tabular machine learning and released these initial results together in April 2024
contributions.quote_verbatim: We benchmarked AIDE on a set of Kaggle tasks focusing on tabular machine learning and released these initial results together in April 2024

contributions.statement: Tree-search-based code optimization with LLMs achieves state-of-the-art performance on ML engineering benchmarks, earning twice the medals of the next-best agent on MLE-Bench (with GPT-4o) and surpassing human expert solutions on time-constrained RE-Bench tasks such as Triton Kernel optimization.
contributions.quote: AIDE effectively trades computational resources for enhanced performance, achieving state-of-the-art results on multiple machine learning engineering benchmarks, including our Kaggle evaluations, OpenAI MLE-Bench and METR's RE-Bench … AIDE achieves twice the number of medals compared to a follow-up agent … AIDE's final solution surpasses that of human experts, even when the latter had extended development time.
contributions.quote_verbatim: ons, AIDE effectively trades computational resources for enhanced performance, achieving state-of-the-art results on multiple machine learning engineering benchmarks, including our Kaggle evaluations, OpenAI’s MLE-Bench and METR’s RE-Bench. The implementation of AIDE is publicly available at https://github.com/WecoAI/aideml . 1 Introduction Machine learning engineering supports many modern AI achievements, from

contributions.statement: GPT-4-generated peer reviews achieve comparable mean helpfulness ratings to human reviews (~3/5), but with higher variance, suggesting AI can contribute to academic peer review while remaining inconsistent in quality.
contributions.quote: our findings open new avenues for leveraging machine learning tools to address resource constraints in peer review
contributions.quote_verbatim: Our findings open new avenues for leveraging machine learning tools to address resource constraints in peer review

contributions.statement: A three-step prompting pipeline for automated peer review — generating section-level notes, organizing them into a structured format, and producing a guideline-compliant review — enables GPT-4 to produce formatted conference-style reviews.
contributions.quote: Our approach works in the three steps. First, we have GPT generate notes on different sections of the paper that are helpful for generating a review. Second, we have GPT organize these notes into a consistent format. Third, we give GPT formal guidelines for generating a review and then have it generate a review based on the organized notes.
contributions.quote_verbatim: le of producing coherent reviews. . First, we have GPT generate notes on different sections of the paper that are helpful for generating a review. Second, we have GPT organize these notes into a consistent format. Third, we give GPT formal guidelines for generating a review and then have it generate a review based on the organized notes. We

contributions.statement: GPT-4 reliably detects negated abstract claims but struggles to flag informality errors inserted at the sentence level, and performs better with smaller context windows (4k vs 32k tokens) on these adversarial tasks.
contributions.quote: we found that GPT-4 was able to identify and raise concerns consistently when the abstract was rewritten, but struggled more with identifying errors inserted at the sentence level. We also find that GPT-4 demonstrated stronger performance when using a smaller context-size.
contributions.quote_verbatim: we found that GPT-4 was able to identify and raise concerns consistently when the abstract was rewritten, but struggled more with identifying errors inserted at the sentence level. We also find that GPT-4 demonstrated stronger performance when using a smaller context-size.

contributions.statement: MARG (Multi-Agent Review Generation) — a system in which multiple LLM instances each receive a portion of a scientific paper, engage in structured internal discussion, and collectively generate peer-review feedback for papers whose full text exceeds any single LLM's context window.
contributions.quote: We propose a novel method (MARG) that can generate high-quality peer-review feedback even for papers longer than the context size of the base model.
contributions.quote_verbatim: We propose a novel method (MARG) that can generate high-quality peer-review feedback even for papers longer than the context size of the base model.

contributions.statement: MARG-S, a specialized variant of MARG that incorporates aspect-specific 'expert' agents for experiments, clarity, and impact sub-tasks, substantially improves the specificity and helpfulness of automatically generated scientific feedback compared to single-agent and non-specialized multi-agent baselines.
contributions.quote: by specializing agents and incorporating sub-tasks tailored to different comment types (experiments, clarity, impact) it improves the helpfulness and specificity of feedback … we refer to this specialized variant of our method as MARG-S
contributions.quote_verbatim: by specializing agents and incorporating sub-tasks tailored to different comment types (experiments, clarity, impact) it improves the helpfulness and specificity of feedback. In a user study, baseline methods using GPT-4 were rated as

contributions.statement: Distributing long document text across multiple cooperating LLM agents is an effective strategy for context management that retains full document fidelity, outperforming summarization and truncation approaches for tasks where nuanced details throughout the document are critical.
contributions.quote: we divide the input among multiple agents that collectively retain the full text throughout the task … our method outperforms the strongest baseline by 6.1 recall points in the automated evaluation and generates 2.2x as many helpful comments per review in the user study
contributions.quote_verbatim: generated feedback against two baselines, using both automatic metrics and a user study. We find that our method outperforms the strongest baseline by 6.1 recall points in the automated evaluation and generates 2.2x as many helpful comments per review in the user study

contributions.statement: Specializing LLM agents by comment type reduces the rate of generic peer-review comments from 60% to 29% and increases the count of user-rated 'good' comments from 1.7 to 3.7 per paper (a 2.2× improvement) over strong GPT-4 baselines.
contributions.quote: Our system substantially improves the ability of GPT-4 to generate specific and helpful feedback, reducing the rate of generic comments from 60% to 29% and generating 3.7 good comments per paper (a 2.2x improvement)
contributions.quote_verbatim: Our system substantially improves the ability of GPT-4 to generate specific and helpful feedback, reducing the rate of generic comments from 60% to 29% and generating 3.7 good comments per paper (a 2.2x improvement)

contributions.statement: A failure analysis of multi-agent scientific review generation identifies internal communication errors (e.g., omission of key information across agent messages) and high cost as primary weaknesses, characterizing the error modes of the approach.
contributions.quote: we analyze the weaknesses of MARG-S, including high cost and internal communication errors (e.g., failing to include key information in some messages)
contributions.quote_verbatim: we analyze the weaknesses of MARG-S , including high cost and internal communication errors (e.g., failing to include key information in some messages

contributions.statement: AgentReview — an LLM agent-based simulation framework that models the full peer review pipeline (reviewer assessment, author rebuttal, area-chair discussion, meta-review, and final decision) with customizable reviewer, author, and area-chair characteristics — enables controlled disentanglement of latent variables in peer review without requiring access to sensitive real-world data.
contributions.quote: AGENTREVIEW is the first framework to employ LLM agents to simulate the entire peer review process
contributions.quote_verbatim:
AGENTREVIEW is the first
framework to employ LLM agents to simulate
the entire peer review process

contributions.statement: A large-scale synthetic peer review corpus of over 53,800 documents — including initial reviews, rebuttals, updated reviews, meta-reviews, and final decisions — generated across more than 500 ICLR submissions spanning four years, supports future computational research on academic peer review.
contributions.quote: We curated a large-scale dataset through our simulation, encompassing more than 53,800 generated reviews, rebuttals, updated reviews, meta-reviews, and final decisions, which can support future research on analyzing the academic peer review process
contributions.quote_verbatim:

contributions.statement: Reviewer biases alone account for a 37.1% variation in paper acceptance decisions, with specific mechanisms including social conformity pressure (27.2% reduction in rating variance post-rebuttal), altruism fatigue cascades (18.7% commitment decline from a single under-committed reviewer), authority and halo effects (27.7% decision shift when author identities are known), and groupthink amplification among biased reviewers — findings corroborated by established sociological theories.
contributions.quote: Our study uncovers several significant findings that align with sociological theories to support future research; including a notable 37.1% variation in paper decisions due to reviewers' biases, supported by sociological theories such as the social influence theory, altruism fatigue, and authority bias
contributions.quote_verbatim:

and authority bias. W

contributions.statement: SWIF2T (Scientific WrIting Focused Feedback Tool) — a four-component system (planner, investigator, reviewer, controller) using multiple LLMs — generates specific, actionable, and coherent focused feedback that identifies weaknesses in scientific paper paragraphs and proposes revisions, outperforming GPT-4 and CoVe baselines in specificity, reading comprehension, and overall helpfulness.
contributions.quote: we present SWIF2T, a Scientific WrIting Focused Feedback Tool. This approach leverages multiple LLMs to decompose the process of generating scientific feedback into four components (Figure 2): planner, investigator, reviewer and controller.
contributions.quote_verbatim:
present SWIF2T, a Scientific
WrIting Focused Feedback Tool. This approach
leverages multiple LLMs to decompose the process
of generating scientific feedback into four compo-
nents (Figure 2): planner, investigator, reviewer
and controller.

contributions.statement: Automated focused feedback generation is a well-defined task distinct from high-level review generation: given a specific paragraph within a scientific paper, the goal is to produce comments that demonstrate reading comprehension, specificity, and actionability toward improving research content.
contributions.quote: In this paper, we introduce the task of automated focused feedback generation for scientific writing assistance (Figure 1). Focused feedback entails providing specific, actionable, and coherent comments that identify weaknesses in a paper or suggest revisions.
contributions.quote_verbatim:

contributions.statement: A plan re-ranking method for multi-step LLM-based feedback pipelines improves the coherence, structure, and specificity of generated review plans, thereby enhancing downstream scientific feedback quality.
contributions.quote: we also propose a plan re-ranking method aimed at optimizing the coherence, structure, and specificity of the generated plan, thereby enhancing the effectiveness of our scientific feedback generation process.
contributions.quote_verbatim:
e also
propose a plan re-ranking method aimed at optimiz-
ing the coherence, structure, and specificity of the
generated plan, thereby enhancing the effectiveness
of our scientific feedback generation process

contributions.statement: A benchmark dataset of 300 human-written peer-review examples, each linking a weakness-citing review comment to a specific paragraph of a scientific paper, enables evaluation of focused scientific feedback generation systems.
contributions.quote: we compile a test set using human-written feedback derived from peer reviews. It consists of 300 examples where reviews highlight weaknesses in specific paragraphs of scientific papers.
contributions.quote_verbatim:

human-written feedback derived from peer reviews.

weaknesses in specific paragraphs of scientific pa-
pers.

contributions.statement: Automatically generated scientific feedback can in some cases be judged superior to human-written peer reviews, indicating practical potential for AI integration in the scientific writing process.
contributions.quote: Our analysis reveals instances where SWIF2T-generated comments are preferred over human-written ones, hinting at the potential for integration of AI-generated feedback in the scientific writing process.
contributions.quote_verbatim:
r analysis reveals instances where
SWIF2T -generated comments are preferred over
human-written ones, hinting at the potential for in-
tegration of AI-generated feedback in the scientific
writing process

contributions.statement: Generative Agent Reviewers (GAR) — an LLM-agent framework with profile, memory, novelty, and review modules — automates academic peer review, providing detailed, on-demand, expert-level feedback and acceptance-likelihood predictions comparable to human reviewers.
contributions.quote: We introduce GAR, a framework that automates peer review using LLM agents, providing detailed, on-demand feedback to researchers.
contributions.quote_verbatim: We introduce GAR, a framework that automates peer review using LLM agents, providing detailed, on-demand feedback to researchers.

contributions.statement: A contrastive-comparison technique extracts fine-grained reviewer personas (e.g., strictness, focus areas) from a single historical review, enabling LLM agents to faithfully replicate individual reviewer styles.
contributions.quote: We propose a novel technique to extract reviewer personas from a single review based on contrastive comparison.
contributions.quote_verbatim: We propose a novel technique to extract reviewer personas from a single review based on contrastive comparison.

contributions.statement: A graph-based representation of academic manuscripts organizes content by linking ideas, claims, evidence, and technical details, improving coherence and depth in automated paper evaluation.
contributions.quote: We present a novel graph-based representation of academic documents, designed to capture relationships between ideas, claims and results.
contributions.quote_verbatim: We present a novel graph-based representation of academic documents, designed to capture relationships between ideas, claims and results.

contributions.statement: A multi-round review process — where an agent produces an initial review and iteratively refines it via memory retrieval and self-assessment — better emulates the iterative nature of real-world peer review.
contributions.quote: We introduce a multi-round review process, wherein the agent generates an initial review and iteratively refines the review based on retrieved information from the memory module and self-assessment.
contributions.quote_verbatim: We introduce a multi-round review process, wherein the agent generates an initial review and iteratively refines the review based on retrieved information from the memory module and self-assessment.

contributions.statement: A meta-reviewer agent that aggregates multiple LLM-generated individual reviews into a single, consistent final acceptance decision improves prediction accuracy over individual synthetic reviews.
contributions.quote: We propose a meta-reviewer agent that synthesizes individual reviews into a final, consistent decision.
contributions.quote_verbatim: We propose a meta-reviewer agent that synthesizes individual reviews into a final, consistent decision.

contributions.statement: LLM-generated excerpt highlights (annotations) serve as a viable middle ground for AI-human collaboration in academic peer review, allowing LLMs to flag relevant passages without replacing the critical judgment of human reviewers.
contributions.quote: making a case for annotation as a suitable middle ground between human reviewers and LLM interaction.
contributions.quote_verbatim: making a case for annotation as a suitable middle ground between human reviewers and LLM interaction.

contributions.statement: AnnotateGPT — a dedicated web platform that uses GPT-4 to automatically annotate research manuscripts with criterion-linked excerpt highlights before a human reviewer begins reading — improves reviewer comprehension and focus.
contributions.quote: providing proof-of-concept through AnnotateGPT, a dedicated platform for manuscript review with GPT-4 as the LLM
contributions.quote_verbatim: roviding proof-of-concept through AnnotateGPT , a dedicated platform for manuscript review with GPT-4 as the LLM

contributions.statement: A Technology Acceptance Model (TAM) evaluation of AI-generated manuscript annotations (n=9) shows perceived usefulness and ease of use sufficient for adoption, with findings generalized across reviewer and editor stakeholder roles.
contributions.quote: evaluating AnnotateGPT through a TAM questionnaire (n=9) and generalizing the results to other stakeholders
contributions.quote_verbatim: evaluating AnnotateGPT through a TAM questionnaire (n=9) and generalizing the results to other stakeholders

contributions.statement: Llama-OpenReviewer-8B — an 8B-parameter language model fine-tuned on 79,000 expert peer reviews from top ML/AI conferences (ICLR, NeurIPS) — generates structured, critical academic reviews that closely match human reviewer recommendation distributions.
contributions.quote: A specialized long-context large language model for generating academic reviews, finetuned on a large dataset of expert reviews from top ML conferences.
contributions.quote_verbatim: A specialized long-context large language model for generating academic reviews, finetuned on a large dataset of expert reviews from top ML conferences.

contributions.statement: OpenReviewer — an open-source system combining transformer-based PDF processing with a domain-specialized language model — generates comprehensive, conference-template-following peer reviews directly from paper PDF submissions.
contributions.quote: An open-source demo that combines state-of-the-art transformer-based PDF processing with our specialized model to generate comprehensive reviews from paper submissions.
contributions.quote_verbatim: An open-source demo that combines state-of-the-art transformer-based PDF processing with our specialized model to generate comprehensive reviews from paper submissions.

contributions.statement: General-purpose LLMs (GPT-4o, Claude-3.5-Sonnet) are systematically too lenient as paper reviewers, producing recommendation distributions far more positive than human expert reviewers, while domain fine-tuning on expert review data substantially closes this gap.
contributions.quote: An evaluation demonstrating that OpenReviewer generates reviews that align considerably better with human expert reviews compared to state-of-the-art LLMs, including GPT-4o and Claude-3.5-Sonnet. We find that general-purpose LLMs are not critical enough and tend to give much more positive recommendations than human reviews.
contributions.quote_verbatim: An evaluation demonstrating that OpenReviewer generates reviews that align considerably better with human expert reviews compared to state-of-the-art LLMs, including GPT-4o and Claude-3.5-Sonnet. We find that general-purpose LLMs are not critical enough and tend to give much more positive recommendations than human reviews.

contributions.statement: ReviewCritique — a dataset of 100 NLP paper submissions paired with both human-written and LLM-generated reviews, where every review is annotated at the sentence level by domain experts (most holding Ph.D. degrees or area-chairing experience) for deficiency labels and explanations — supports benchmarking of AI-assisted peer review and LLM meta-reviewing capabilities.
contributions.quote: the ReviewCritique dataset with human-written and LLM-generated reviews and fine-grained review deficiency labeling and explanation, serving as a valuable resource for future research on AI-assisted peer review and LLM benchmarking
contributions.quote_verbatim:

assisted peer review and LLM benchmarking

contributions.statement: A novel metric for measuring diversity of LLM-generated paper reviews across different submissions quantifies the degree to which generated reviews are paper-specific versus generic.
contributions.quote: We propose a novel metric to measure LLM-generated review diversity across different papers.

pro-
pose a novel metric to measure LLM-generated re-
view diversity across different paper

contributions.statement: At the sentence level, LLM-generated paper reviews contain more deficient segments than human-written reviews and tend to be paper-unspecific, lacking diversity and constructive feedback — establishing a quantitative gap between human and LLM reviewing quality.
contributions.quote: the first quantitative comparison of human-written and LLM-generated paper reviews at the sentence level … Our findings indicate that LLMs generate more Deficient review segments than human reviewers and often produce paper-unspecific reviews lacking diversity and constructive feedback.
