contributions.statement: Even state-of-the-art closed-source and open-source LLMs fall substantially short of human expert meta-reviewers when tasked with identifying deficient or unprofessional segments within individual paper reviews, demonstrating that meta-reviewing requires a level of expertise and nuanced judgment current LLMs cannot replicate.
contributions.quote: We benchmark both closed-source and open-source LLMs on this task, finding that even top-tier LLMs struggle to mimic human experts in assessing individual reviews.
contributions.quote_verbatim:
benchmark both closed-source
and open-source LLMs on this task, finding that
even top-tier LLMs struggle to mimic human ex-
perts in assessing individual reviews.

contributions.statement: Applying TELeR taxonomy-based prompting with LLMs (GPT-3.5, PaLM2, LLaMA2) to generate controlled multi-perspective summaries (MPS) of peer-reviewer comments is a viable approach to assist human meta-reviewers in comprehending diverse expert opinions more consistently and efficiently.
contributions.quote: we performed a case study using LLMs to generate multi-perspective summaries (MPSs) of reviewers' comments to see whether LLM-generated summaries can assist meta-reviewers in being more consistent and efficient in this important role

contributions.statement: A curated benchmark dataset of 40 ICLR submissions (2020–2023) paired with peer-reviewer comments, expert-written meta-reviews, and 480 LLM-generated multi-perspective summaries annotated with 4,800 granular human judgments across five criteria supports evaluation of LLM-assisted meta-reviewing.
contributions.quote: Our dataset is available at https://github.com/BridgeAI-Lab/LLM-as-Meta-Reviewer
contributions.quote_verbatim:
Our dataset is available at https://github.com/BridgeAI-

contributions.statement: GPT-3.5 and PaLM2 produce multi-perspective summaries of peer reviews of comparable quality—rated higher than LLaMA2 by human evaluators—with PaLM2 yielding better recall and GPT-3.5 yielding better precision at the manuscript level, while GPT-3.5 is rated poorly at the LLM level.
contributions.quote: GPT-3.5 and PaLM2 performed comparably and were rated higher by humans than LLaMA2 in terms of manuscript-level judgments. Interestingly, PaLM2 generally yielded better recall scores, while GPT-3.5 yielded better precision scores. More surprisingly, GPT-3.5 was rated poorly by humans at LLM-level judgments

contributions.statement: GPT-4-based automatic evaluation correlates poorly with human judgments when assessing multi-perspective summaries, indicating that LLM-based evaluation is unreliable for aspect-aware summarization tasks involving narratives from multiple diverse viewpoints.
contributions.quote: the automatic evaluation shows that the GPT-4 evaluation correlates poorly with human judgments when tasked with evaluating the meta-reviews generated by its predecessors (i.e., GPT-3.5). Our findings also highlight that LLM-based evaluations are unreliable in tasks that require aspect-aware summary evaluation, especially when dealing with narratives from multiple diverse perspectives

contributions.statement: SciSciGPT — an open-source, multi-agent LLM-powered research collaborator for the science of science — automates complex empirical workflows, supports data extraction and analytics, generates visualizations, and enables low-code/no-code research prototyping and reproducibility through a hierarchical five-agent architecture (ResearchManager, LiteratureSpecialist, DatabaseSpecialist, AnalyticsSpecialist, EvaluationSpecialist).
contributions.quote: We introduce SciSciGPT, an open-source, prototype artificial intelligence (AI) collaborator that uses the domain of science of science as a testbed to explore the potential of large language model-powered research tools. SciSciGPT automates complex workflows, supports diverse analytical approaches, accelerates research prototyping and iteration and facilitates reproducibility.

contributions.statement: An LLM agent capability maturity model for human–AI collaboration structures the development roadmap for AI research collaborators across four levels — functional capabilities, workflow orchestration, memory architecture, and human–AI collaborative paradigms — providing a principled framework for evaluating and advancing agentic AI systems in research environments.
contributions.quote: We further propose a large language model agent capability maturity model for human–AI collaboration, envisioning a roadmap to further improve and expand upon frameworks such as SciSciGPT … the proposed maturity model provides a framework to guide further developments and extensions, offering a strong foundation for agentic AI system development across broad research environments.
contributions.quote_verbatim:

human–AI collaborative paradigms. As a proof of concept of the

tures from the model, and the proposed maturity model provides a 
framework to guide further developments and extensions, offering 
a strong foundation for agentic AI system development across broad 
research environments.

contributions.statement: A multi-agent LLM framework — comprising code-writer, code-reviewer, and vision agents — can orchestrate complex, multimodal, multi-step experimental workflows at advanced scientific user facilities, demonstrated on an X-ray nanoprobe beamline and an autonomous robotic materials-design station.
contributions.quote: we develop and benchmark a multi-agent framework that operates complex scientific instrumentation at two distinct scientific user facilities, a synchrotron X-ray nanoprobe beamline and an autonomous robotic station for iterative materials design. The agents can orchestrate multistep experimental workflows, interpret multimodal data streams and interactively collaborate with human researchers.
contributions.quote_verbatim:

collaborate with human research

contributions.statement: On-the-job learning for scientific instrument agents is achievable through two complementary in-context learning mechanisms: expert-designed prompt templates encoding experimental heuristics for standardized workflows, and automatic embedding of real-time user instructions as retrievable interaction memories for open-ended workflows.
contributions.quote: We integrate this learning in two ways. For the highly standardized X-ray experiments, the vision agent is provided with expert-designed prompt templates that encode scanning and diffraction analysis heuristics. For the more open-ended robotic workflows, users can explain the operational procedures in real time and each instruction is automatically embedded as a user-interaction memory that the agent can retrieve in future sessions.

contributions.statement: LLMs serving as trainable scientific assistants for instrument control improve performance through optional human feedback and iterative learning, with measurable differences across LLM architectures on multi-task scientific workflows that include multimodal data.
contributions.quote: we evaluate the potential of various LLMs as trainable scientific assistants for orchestrating complex, multi-task workflows, which also include multimodal data, optimizing their performance through optional human input and iterative learning. We evaluated the agents' ability to learn from human scientists and their potential for self-improvement through these interactions.

contributions.statement: SCI-IDEA — a two-stage framework for context-aware scientific ideation that compresses researcher publications into structured facets (objectives, methodology, evaluation, future work, ~200 tokens each), enabling scalable LLM-based idea generation across full researcher profiles that would otherwise exceed context-window limits.
contributions.quote: A scalable, two-stage framework for context-aware scientific ideation that processes researcher papers through structured facet compression, reducing per-paper token requirements and achieving 100% context-window feasibility across all evaluated profiles.
contributions.quote_verbatim:
A scalable, two-stage framework for context-aware scientific ideation that processes re­
searcher papers through structured facet compression, reducing per-paper token require­
ments and achieving 100% context-window feasibility across all evaluated profi

contributions.statement: An Aha-Moment Detection module that jointly scores candidate scientific ideas on novelty (token-level semantic dissimilarity via cosine similarity on embeddings) and surprise (negative log-likelihood under a pretrained LLM), flagging ideas that exceed both thresholds as potentially transformative and routing them to iterative refinement.
contributions.quote: A dual-signal Aha-Moment Detection module that effects novelty via token-level semantic dissimilarity and surprise via likelihood-based scoring, enabling principled identification of potentially transformative ideas beyond iterative planning alone.
contributions.quote_verbatim:
ual-signal Aha-Moment Detection module that effects novelty via token-level se­
mantic dissimilarity and surprise via likelihood-based scoring, enabling principled iden­

contributions.statement: Token-level embeddings yield modest but statistically significant feasibility improvements (+0.21 points, p<0.001) over no-embedding and sentence-level baselines in LLM-driven scientific ideation, and architectural embedding choices have a larger effect than prompt engineering on output usability.
contributions.quote: An empirical investigation across LLM, prompts, and embedding configurations showing that token-level embeddings yield modest but statistically significant improvements in feasibility over both the no embedding baseline and sentence-level embeddings (∆ = +0.21 points, p<0.001), and that architectural choices dominate prompt engineering in driving usability.

contributions.statement: LLM-as-judge scores for open-ended scientific ideation systematically exceed PhD-level expert ratings by 3–4 points on a 10-point scale, with near-zero inter-rater correlation (r=0.02–0.17, p>0.05), indicating that automated evaluation captures relative architectural differences rather than absolute idea quality as judged by domain experts.
contributions.quote: A hybrid evaluation study that explicitly quantifies and reports the variation between automated and expert evaluation, providing transparency about the reliability of LLM-as-judge protocols for open-ended scientific ideation tasks.
contributions.quote_verbatim:
hybrid evaluation study that explicitly quantifies and reports the variation between 
automated and expert evaluation, providing transparency about the reliability of LLM-
as-judge protocols for open-ended scientific ideation tasks

contributions.statement: SDE (Scientific Discovery Evaluation) — a two-phase benchmark framework in which domain experts decompose genuine research projects into modular scenarios and evaluate LLMs at both question-level accuracy and project-level performance (hypothesis proposal, experiment/simulation design, and result interpretation) across biology, chemistry, materials science, and physics.
contributions.quote: Here, we introduce a scenario-grounded benchmark that evaluates LLMs across biology, chemistry, material science, and physics. For this benchmark, domain experts define research projects of genuine value and interest, and decompose them into modular research scenarios from which vetted questions are sampled. The framework assesses models at two levels: (i) question-level accuracy on scenario-tied items and (ii) project-level performance, where models must propose testable hypotheses, execute simulations or experiments, and interpret results.
contributions.quote_verbatim: Here, we introduce a scenario-grounded benchmark that evaluates LLMs across biology, chemistry, material science, and physics. For this benchmark, domain experts define research projects of genuine value and interest, and decompose them into modular research scenarios from which vetted questions are sampled. The framework assesses models at two levels: (i) question-level accuracy on scenario-tied items and (ii) project-level performance, where models must propose testable hypotheses, execute simulations or experiments, and interpret results.

contributions.statement: Applying the SDE framework to state-of-the-art LLMs reveals a consistent performance gap relative to general science benchmarks, diminishing returns from scaling model size and reasoning, and systematic weaknesses shared across top-tier models from different providers, indicating all current LLMs remain far from general scientific 'superintelligence'.
contributions.quote: Applying this two-phase scientific discovery evaluation (SDE) framework to state-of-the-art LLMs reveals a consistent performance gap relative to general science benchmarks, diminishing return of scaling up model sizes and reasoning, and systematic weaknesses shared across top-tier models from different providers.
contributions.quote_verbatim: pplying this two-phase scientific discovery evaluation ( SDE ) framework to state-of-the-art LLMs reveals a consistent performance gap relative to general science benchmarks, diminishing return of scaling up model sizes and reasoning, and systematic weaknesses shared across top-tier models from different providers

contributions.statement: High scenario-level scores do not reliably predict project-level success: LLMs can succeed on full discovery projects even when constituent scenario scores are low, highlighting the role of guided exploration and serendipity in LLM-assisted scientific discovery.
contributions.quote: LLMs already demonstrate promise in a great variety of scientific discovery projects, including cases where constituent scenario scores are low, highlighting the role of guided exploration and serendipity in discovery.
contributions.quote_verbatim: LLMs already demonstrate promise in a great variety of scientific discovery projects, including cases where constituent scenario scores are low, highlighting the role of guided exploration and serendipity in discovery.

contributions.statement: Large performance variation across research scenarios causes the identity of the best-performing model to change depending on the scientific discovery project evaluated, meaning no single LLM dominates across all projects.
contributions.quote: Large performance variation in research scenarios leads to changing choices of the best performing model on scientific discovery projects evaluated, suggesting all current LLMs are distant to general scientific 'superintelligence'.
contributions.quote_verbatim: Large performance variation in research scenarios leads to changing choices of the best performing model on scientific discovery projects evaluated, suggesting all current LLMs are distant to general scientific “superintelligence”.

contributions.statement: ResearchGym — an extensible execution environment and benchmark of five containerized tasks (39 sub-tasks) derived from recent oral/spotlight papers at ICML, ICLR, and ACL — enables objective, execution-based evaluation of AI agents on closed-loop research, including hypothesis generation, experimentation, and surpassing human baselines, while remaining feasible on a single GPU.
contributions.quote: An extensible execution environment for agent/task integration and objective grading (§ 2.3). A benchmark of five tasks with 39 sub-tasks for closed-loop research evaluation with contamination-aware construction and single-GPU accessibility (§ 2.2).
contributions.quote_verbatim: An extensible execution environment for agent/task integration and objective grading (§ 2.3 ). • A benchmark of five tasks with 39 sub-tasks for closed-loop research evaluation with contamination-aware construction and single-GPU accessibility (§ 2.

contributions.statement: Frontier AI agents exhibit a sharp capability–reliability gap on end-to-end research tasks: a GPT-5-based agent surpasses provided baselines in only 1 of 15 evaluations (6.7%) and completes 26.5% of sub-tasks on average, yet in one run exceeds the human reference solution of an ICML 2025 Spotlight task, showing occasional but unreliable state-of-the-art performance.
contributions.quote: the agent improves over the provided baselines from the repository in just 1 of 15 evaluations (6.7%) by 11.5%, and completes only 26.5% of sub-tasks on average … Yet in a single run, the agent surpasses the solution of an ICML 2025 Spotlight task, indicating that frontier agents can occasionally reach state-of-the-art performance, but do so unreliably.
contributions.quote_verbatim: improves over provided baselines in only 1 run (6.7%) and completes just 26.5% of sub-tasks on average, with performance plateauing after 9 hours. Yet this single successful run outperforms the human reference solution on an ICML 2025 Spotlight task, demonstrating that current frontier agents can occasionally reach state-of-the-art, but do so unreliably

contributions.statement: Recurring long-horizon failure modes in autonomous research agents include impatience, poor time and resource management, overconfidence in weak hypotheses, difficulty coordinating parallel experiments, and context-length limits, as identified through ablations, 35+ end-to-end runs, and qualitative case studies.
contributions.quote: A controlled evaluation with ablations and 35+ end-to-end runs, failure-mode analysis, time–token performance tradeoffs, and case studies (§ 4, § 5).
contributions.quote_verbatim: controlled evaluation with ablations and 35+ end-to-end runs, failure-mode analysis, time–token performance tradeoffs, and case studies (§ 4 , § 5 ).

contributions.statement: ScholarGym — an evaluation environment that isolates and decomposes the information-gathering stage of deep research into three explicit, separately scorable stages (Query Planning, Tool Invocation, and Relevance Assessment) executed over a static corpus with deterministic retrieval, enabling reproducible and decomposable analysis of LLM behavior in long-horizon academic literature retrieval.
contributions.quote: We introduce ScholarGym, an evaluation environment that isolates the information-gathering stage of deep research on academic literature. Under a unified workflow, ScholarGym decomposes the research process into three explicit stages—Query Planning, Tool Invocation, and Relevance Assessment—and evaluates each against 2,536 expert-annotated queries over a static corpus of 570K papers with deterministic retrieval.
contributions.quote_verbatim: We introduce ScholarGym , an evaluation environment that isolates the information-gathering stage of deep research on academic literature. Under a unified workflow, ScholarGym decomposes the research process into three explicit stages—Query Planning, Tool Invocation, and Relevance Assessment—and evaluates each against 2,536 expert-annotated queries over a static corpus of 570K papers with deterministic retrieval

contributions.statement: A benchmark dataset of 2,536 expert-annotated research queries paired with a static corpus of 570K academic papers and a memory mechanism that maintains reasoning state across iterations, supporting rigorous, reproducible evaluation of multi-turn academic literature retrieval.
contributions.quote: We construct 2,536 queries with expert-annotated ground truth and equip the workflow with a memory mechanism that maintains reasoning state across iterations, enabling rigorous, reproducible evaluation of long-horizon academic retrieval.
contributions.quote_verbatim: We construct 2,536 queries with expert-annotated ground truth and equip the workflow with a memory mechanism that maintains reasoning state across iterations, enabling rigorous, reproducible evaluation of long-horizon academic retrieval.

contributions.statement: Iterative query decomposition into subqueries across multiple retrieval rounds yields 2.9–3.3× F1 gains over single-query retrieval in academic deep research, confirming the decisive value of iterative planning for information coverage.
contributions.quote: iterative query decomposition yields 2.9–3.3 F1 gains over single-query retrieval, confirming the value of iterative planning.
contributions.quote_verbatim: rching iteratively yields 2.9–3.3 F1 improvements over single-query retrieval, confirming the value of iterative planning. We

contributions.statement: LLMs with extended thinking capabilities systematically trade recall for precision during academic information gathering, producing a measurable precision–recall imbalance relative to standard models.
contributions.quote: models with extended thinking trade recall for precision
contributions.quote_verbatim: models with extended thinking trade recall for precision

contributions.statement: Query Planning quality and Relevance Assessment are dual bottlenecks that separate proprietary from open-source model performance in deep research information gathering, with proprietary models scoring 43% higher on query formulation distance metrics.
contributions.quote: Query Planning quality together with Relevance Assessment constitute dual bottlenecks that separate proprietary from open-source model performance … proprietary models score 43% higher on Avg.Distance, reflecting a marked advantage in query formulation.
contributions.quote_verbatim: We further identify Query Planning quality and Relevance Assessment as dual bottlenecks constraining open-source models: proprietary models score 43% higher on Avg.Distance, reflecting a marked advantage in query formulation. 2 Related Work 2.1 Deep Res

contributions.statement: RE-Bench (Research Engineering Benchmark) — seven open-ended ML research engineering environments with continuous scoring metrics — enables direct, realistic comparison of AI agent R&D capabilities against human expert performance.
contributions.quote: We introduce RE-Bench (Research Engineering Benchmark, v1), which consists of 7 challenging, open-ended ML research engineering environments and data from 71 8-hour attempts by 61 distinct human experts.
contributions.quote_verbatim: infrastructure that require an intervention from us or would take 1hr for a skilled engineer to resolve or work around while doing the environment. Additionally, there are few cases where human experts

contributions.statement: A dataset of 71 eight-hour human expert attempts across seven ML research engineering tasks provides fine-grained human baselines for evaluating AI R&D automation at varying time budgets.
contributions.quote: We provide results from 44 human runs and 35 runs with a basic Claude-3.5-Sonnet agent in these 7 environments, and find that human researchers are slow to start, but make consistent progress over time, while agents outpace humans in the first hour but then plateau. This baselining data allows for fine-grained comparisons between human experts and future AI systems.
contributions.quote_verbatim: results from 44 human runs [f] and 35 runs with a basic Claude-3.5-Sonnet agent in these 7 environments, and find that human researchers are slow to start, but make consistent progress over time, while agents outpace humans in the first hour but then plateau (see Figure 1 ). This baselining data allows for fine-grained comparisons between human experts and future AI

contributions.statement: AI agents outperform human experts at short time budgets (2 hours, by 4×) but humans achieve progressively higher relative scores at longer budgets (matching AI at 8 hours, reaching 2× AI scores at 32 hours), revealing a time-horizon scaling gap in current AI R&D automation.
contributions.quote: the best AI agents achieve a score 4x higher than human experts when both are given a total time budget of 2 hours per environment. However, humans currently display better returns to increasing time budgets, narrowly exceeding the top AI agent scores given an 8-hour budget, and achieving 2x the score of the top AI agent when both are given 32 total hours
contributions.quote_verbatim: The agent has a tendency to make repeated mistaken assumptions when debugging or resolving issues, which often spiraled into total inability to progress in the environment. For example, in one run of “Restricted architecture MLM”, the agent gets stuck dealing with a timeout issue, and spends over an hour making its solution worse and worse trying to get

contributions.statement: Five desiderata for AI R&D evaluation environments — low floor, high ceiling, few ambiguities, novelty/non-memorizability, and broad capability coverage — provide a principled framework for designing reliable early-warning assessments of AI research automation risk.
contributions.quote: We also identify and evaluate 5 key desiderata (such as novelty/non-memorizability, and having a low rate of technical issues and ambiguities) we have aimed for with our AI R&D evaluations, and present a table of how well our environments satisfy them.
contributions.quote_verbatim: We also identify and evaluate 5 key desiderata (such as novelty/non-memorizability, and having a low rate of technical issues and ambiguities) we have aimed for with our AI R D evaluations, and present a table of how well our environments satisfy them.

contributions.statement: Kosmos — an AI scientist system that, given an open-ended research objective and a dataset, autonomously runs iterative cycles of parallel data analysis, literature search, and hypothesis generation for up to 12 hours, then synthesizes findings into fully-cited scientific reports spanning diverse domains including metabolomics, materials science, neuroscience, and statistical genetics.
contributions.quote: Here we present Kosmos, an AI scientist that automates data-driven discovery across a wide range of scientific disciplines. Given an open-ended objective and a dataset, Kosmos performs iterative cycles of parallel data analysis, literature search, and hypothesis generation, and summarizes its discoveries in scientific reports.
contributions.quote_verbatim: Here, we present Kosmos, an AI scientist that automates data-driven discovery across a wide range of scientific disciplines. Given an open-ended objective and a dataset, Kosmos performs iterative cycles of parallel data analysis, literature search, and hypothesis generation, and summarizes its discoveries in scientific reports

contributions.statement: A structured world model for coordinating large numbers of parallel scientific agents enables coherent long-horizon autonomous research — sustaining over 200 agent rollouts that collectively execute an average of 42,000 lines of code and read 1,500 full-length papers per run, an 8× improvement in iteration depth over prior systems.
contributions.quote: The core advancement in Kosmos is the use of a structured world model to manage the output of a large number of agents running in parallel … This context management strategy allows Kosmos to explore many different research avenues simultaneously, and run for eight times as many iterations than existing systems.
contributions.quote_verbatim: the task outputs. Kosmos then queries the world model to propose literature search and data analysis tasks to be completed in the next cycle. This context management strategy allows Kosmos to explore many different research avenues simultaneously, and run for eight times as many iterations than existing systems

contributions.statement: The number of valuable scientific findings produced by an autonomous AI scientist scales linearly with the number of discovery cycles (computational investment), with expert-estimated research equivalence roughly doubling from cycle 5 to cycle 20, reaching an average of 6 months of expert research time per 20-cycle run.
contributions.quote: collaborators reported that a single 20-cycle Kosmos run performed the equivalent of 6 months of their own research time on average. Furthermore, collaborators reported that the number of valuable scientific findings generated scales linearly with Kosmos cycles (tested up to 20 cycles).
contributions.quote_verbatim: ated that the findings from a 20-cycle Kosmos run would have taken them 6.14 months of research to complete (n=7, =2.49), much higher than our estimated time savings given their runs ( Figure 1e ). Furthermore, they report that expert-equivalent research time scales with Kosmos runtime,

contributions.statement: An autonomous AI scientist can independently reproduce unpublished or post-cutoff scientific findings and generate novel, expert-validated discoveries across multiple fields, with 79.4% of report statements verified as accurate by independent domain experts and four out of seven highlighted findings constituting novel contributions to the scientific literature.
contributions.quote: Independent scientists found 79.4% of statements in Kosmos reports to be accurate … Three discoveries independently reproduce findings from preprinted or unpublished manuscripts that were not accessed by Kosmos at runtime, while four make novel contributions to the scientific literature.
contributions.quote_verbatim: hich it originated, ensuring that Kosmos’ reasoning is traceable. We report seven discoveries made by Kosmos: three discoveries made by Kosmos reproduce findings from preprinted or unpublished manuscripts, while the remaining four make novel contributions to the scientific literature. contributions.statement: AIRA_2 — an AI research agent combining asynchronous multi-GPU execution, Hidden Consistent Evaluation, and ReAct operators — achieves a mean Percentile Rank of 81.5% at 24 hours and 83.1% at 72 hours on MLE-bench-30, outperforming the strongest prior baseline (72.7%), and exceeds human state-of-the-art on 6 of 20 tasks on AIRS-Bench.
contributions.quote: We introduce AIRA_2, which addresses these bottlenecks through three architectural choices: an asynchronous multi-GPU worker pool that increases experiment throughput linearly; a Hidden Consistent Evaluation protocol that delivers a reliable evaluation signal; and ReAct agents that dynamically scope their actions and debug interactively.
contributions.quote_verbatim: We introduce AIRA , which addresses these bottlenecks through three architectural choices: an asynchronous multi-GPU worker pool that increases experiment throughput linearly; a Hidden Consistent Evaluation protocol that delivers a reliable evaluation signal; and ReAct agents that dynamically scope their actions and debug interactively.

contributions.statement: An asynchronous multi-GPU worker pool with containerization decouples LLM decision-making from experiment execution in AI research agents, increasing candidate-solution throughput linearly with GPU count and making evolutionary search over ML solutions practically tractable within wall-clock budgets.
contributions.quote: AIRA_2 introduces an asynchronous multi-GPU worker pool and containerization system that decouples decision-making from execution, enabling massively parallel experimentation and increasing experiment throughput linearly with available GPU resources.
contributions.quote_verbatim: AIRA introduces an asynchronous multi-GPU worker pool and containerization system that decouples decision-making from execution, enabling massively parallel experimentation and increasing experiment throughput linearly with available GPU resources.

contributions.statement: Hidden Consistent Evaluation (HCE) — standardizing data splits once across all candidates, hiding evaluation labels from the agent, and decoupling the search signal from the final selection signal — eliminates validation-test divergence and enables continued performance gains over extended (72-hour) search horizons.
contributions.quote: AIRA_2 addresses this overfitting with a Hidden Consistent Evaluation (HCE) protocol: data splits are standardized once and reused across all candidates, evaluation labels are hidden from the agent, and the search signal is decoupled from the final selection signal.
contributions.quote_verbatim: AIRA addresses this overfitting with a Hidden Consistent Evaluation (HCE) protocol : data splits are standardized once and reused across all candidates, evaluation labels are hidden from the agent, and the search signal is decoupled from the final selection signal.

contributions.statement: Replacing fixed single-turn LLM operators with ReAct agents that autonomously scope exploratory data analysis, iterative debugging, and log inspection removes the operator-capability ceiling in agentic research systems, improving performance and accelerating discovery of strong solutions.
contributions.quote: AIRA_2 replaces all operators with ReAct agents that autonomously scope their actions—performing exploratory data analysis, running small development experiments, inspecting logs, and more, thus alleviating the limitations of operator design.
contributions.quote_verbatim: places all operators with ReAct agents ( yao2022react ) that autonomously scope their actions—performing exploratory data analysis, running small development experiments, inspecting logs, and more, thus alleviating the limitations of operator

contributions.statement: The performance degradation attributed to 'overfitting' in prior AI research agents over extended search horizons is caused by evaluation noise rather than true data memorization, as confirmed by ablation studies isolating the effect of evaluation consistency.
contributions.quote: Ablations confirm that each architectural component is necessary, that performance follows a predictable scaling law that transfers across LLM backbones, and that the 'overfitting' reported in prior work was driven by evaluation noise rather than true data memorization.
contributions.quote_verbatim: Ablations confirm that each architectural component is necessary, that performance follows a predictable scaling law that transfers across LLM backbones, and that the “overfitting” reported in prior work was driven by evaluation noise rather than true data memorization.

contributions.statement: ScholarPeer — a multi-agent automated peer-review framework that structurally decouples paper contextualization from critique by orchestrating a sub-domain historian, a baseline scout, and a multi-aspect Q&A engine to perform active, search-grounded interrogation of submissions.
contributions.quote: We propose ScholarPeer, a multi-agent framework that performs active, search-grounded interrogation, designed to accelerate author iteration and augment human reviewer judgment.
contributions.quote_verbatim: We propose ScholarPeer , a multi-agent framework that performs active, search-grounded interrogation, designed to accelerate author iteration and augment human reviewer judgment

contributions.statement: Specialized historian, baseline scout, and multi-aspect Q&A agents that jointly perform internal logical-consistency checks and external web-scale verification of claims against top-tier academic venues, enabling verifiable peer-review critique.
contributions.quote: We introduce specialized historian, baseline scout, and Q&A agents that collectively perform both internal logical consistency checks and external web-scale verification against top-tier academic venues.
contributions.quote_verbatim: e introduce specialized historian , baseline scout , and Q A agents that collectively perform both internal logical consistency checks and external web-scale verification against top-tier academic venues

contributions.statement: ScholarEval — a benchmark of ~1,800 ICLR submissions spanning 2020–2025, assembled by merging the DeepReview-Bench and AgentReview evaluation sets, for evaluating automated peer-review systems.
contributions.quote: We comprehensively evaluate ScholarPeer on 1,800 submissions from ICLR 2020 through 2025, merging the evaluation sets of DeepReview-Bench and AgentReview. We call this data set ScholarEval.
contributions.quote_verbatim: comprehensively evaluate ScholarPeer on 1,800 submissions from ICLR 2020 through 2025, merging the evaluation sets of DeepReview-Bench ( zhu2025deepreview ) and AgentReview ( jin2024agentre

contributions.statement: H-Max score — an evaluation metric that calibrates the localized quality of automated peer-review critiques against the best available human expert review.
contributions.quote: we also explore two new evaluation metrics: the H-Max score, which calibrates the localized quality of critiques against human experts, and the review diversity score, which assesses the variance of perspectives provided by the system.
contributions.quote_verbatim: we also explore two new evaluation metrics: the H-Max score , which calibrates the localized quality of critiques against human experts, and the review diversity score , which assesses the variance of perspectives provided by the syste

contributions.statement: Automated peer-review generation at practical deployment cost (~$1.20 and ~10 minutes per review) with dominant win-rates over state-of-the-art fine-tuned models and search-augmented agentic baselines on ~1,800 ICLR submissions.
contributions.quote: We conduct comprehensive evaluations, achieving dominant win-rates against state-of-the-art fine-tuned models and search-enabled agentic baselines, with practical deployment metrics ($1.20 and latency of 10 minutes per review).
contributions.quote_verbatim: e conduct comprehensive evaluations, achieving dominant win-rates against state-of-the-art fine-tuned models and search-enabled agentic baselines, with practical deployment metrics ( $1.20 and latency of 10 minutes per review).

contributions.statement: BioKGBench — a benchmark for evaluating biomedical AI agents that decomposes literature understanding into two atomic subtasks (Knowledge Graph Question Answering and Scientific Claim Verification) and combines them into a novel agent task (KGCheck) for detecting factual errors in large-scale biomedical knowledge graphs.
contributions.quote: we propose a novel agent evaluation benchmark BioKGBench to address both challenges simultaneously... the ultimate goal of our benchmark is to verify the correctness of nodes and triples in the knowledge graph based on various information, including papers and well-maintained databases. We dub this task Knowledge Graph Checking (KGCheck).
contributions.quote_verbatim: ovel agent evaluation benchmark BioKGBench to address both challenges simultaneously. As in Figure 1 (right), the ultimate goal of our benchmark is to verify the correctness of nodes and triples in the knowledge graph based on various information, including papers and well-maintained databases. We dub this task Knowledge Graph Checking (K

contributions.statement: KGCheck — a task formulation that uses Knowledge Graph Question Answering and domain-based Retrieval-Augmented Generation to identify factual errors in existing large-scale biomedical knowledge graph databases.
contributions.quote: We then formulate a novel agent task, dubbed KGCheck, using KGQA and domain-based Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) to identify the factual errors of existing large-scale knowledge graph databases.
contributions.quote_verbatim: We then formulate a novel agent task, dubbed KGCheck, using KGQA and domain-based Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) to identify the factual errors of existing large-scale knowledge graph databases.

contributions.statement: BKGAgent — a baseline agent framework that integrates interaction with external biomedical knowledge graphs and research papers, capable of discovering real factual conflicts in large-scale biomedical datasets such as the Clinical Knowledge Graph.
contributions.quote: we introduce our agent BKGAgent, the first agent framework to interact with external knowledge graphs as well as research papers. Experiments demonstrate fascinating results that our agent is capable of discovering real conflicts in the existing large-scale datasets.
contributions.quote_verbatim: we introduce our agent BKGAgent, the first agent framework to interact with external knowledge graphs as well as research papers. Experiments demonstrate fascinating results that our agent is capable of discovering real conflicts in the existing large-scale datasets.

contributions.statement: State-of-the-art agents — including both general-purpose and biomedical-domain agents — fail or perform inferiorly on biomedical knowledge graph checking tasks, revealing a critical gap in existing agent capabilities for AI Scientist applications.
contributions.quote: Surprisingly, we discover that state-of-the-art agents, both daily scenarios and biomedical ones, have either failed or inferior performance on our benchmark.
contributions.quote_verbatim: Surprisingly, we discover that state-of-the-art agents, both daily scenarios and biomedical ones, have either failed or inferior performance on our benchmark.

contributions.statement: Over 90 factual errors are identified in a widely used biomedical knowledge graph (Clinical Knowledge Graph) by applying the KGCheck pipeline, demonstrating the real-world utility of automated knowledge graph verification.
contributions.quote: On the widely used popular knowledge graph, we discover over 90 factual errors which provide scenarios for agents to make discoveries and demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach.
contributions.quote_verbatim: On the widely used popular knowledge graph, we discover over 90 factual errors which provide scenarios for agents to make discoveries and demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach.

contributions.statement: Verified QA data synthesis for deep research agents — combining adversarial answer-uniqueness verification in graph-based synthesis and multi-stage quality verification with diagnosis-iterative optimization in agent-based web-exploration synthesis — produces question-answer pairs with controlled difficulty, uniqueness, and correctness.
contributions.quote: We introduce verification mechanisms to graph-based and agent-based QA synthesis to control question difficulty while ensuring answers are unique and correct
contributions.quote_verbatim: We introduce verification mechanisms to graph-based and agent-based QA synthesis to control question difficulty while ensuring answers are unique and correct

contributions.statement: Verification-driven trajectory construction injects explicit verification patterns — via a specialized verifier agent that checks sub-task and final answers using web search, plus re-rollout on failed trajectories — into single-agent and multi-agent training trajectories, training models to resist premature answer acceptance in long-horizon search.
contributions.quote: We design a verification-driven trajectory synthesis method that injects explicit verification patterns into training trajectories
contributions.quote_verbatim: We design a verification-driven trajectory synthesis method that injects explicit verification patterns into training trajectories

contributions.statement: Verifier-guided test-time scaling, in which the deep research agent itself serves as its own verifier during inference and continues reasoning on challenging questions under a controlled tool-call budget, unlocks effective test-time compute scaling without a separate verifier model.
contributions.quote: We use Marco DeepResearch itself as a verifier at inference time and effectively improve performance on challenging questions
contributions.quote_verbatim: We use Marco DeepResearch itself as a verifier at inference time and effectively improve performance on challenging questions

contributions.statement: Marco DeepResearch — an 8B-scale deep research agent built on Qwen3-8B and trained with a verification-centric framework — outperforms other 8B-scale deep research agents on benchmarks such as BrowseComp and BrowseComp-ZH, and matches or surpasses several 30B-scale agents within a budget of 600 tool calls.
contributions.quote: our proposed Marco DeepResearch agent significantly outperforms 8B-scale deep research agents on most challenging benchmarks, such as BrowseComp and BrowseComp-ZH. Crucially, under a maximum budget of 600 tool calls, Marco DeepResearch even surpasses or approaches several 30B-scale agents
contributions.quote_verbatim: our proposed Marco DeepResearch agent significantly outperforms 8B-scale deep research agents on most challenging benchmarks, such as BrowseComp and BrowseComp-ZH. Crucially, under a maximum budget of 600 tool calls, Marco DeepResearch even surpasses or approaches several 30B-scale agents

contributions.statement: ToolUniverse — an open ecosystem providing over 600 machine learning models, datasets, APIs, and scientific packages — enables any language or reasoning model (open- or closed-weight) to be configured as an AI scientist without additional training or fine-tuning.
contributions.quote: We present ToolUniverse, an ecosystem for building AI scientists from any language or reasoning model across open- and closed-weight models. ToolUniverse standardizes how AI scientists identify and call tools by providing more than 600 machine learning models, datasets, APIs, and scientific packages for data analysis, knowledge retrieval, and experimental design.
contributions.quote_verbatim: We present ToolUniverse , an ecosystem for building AI scientists from any language or reasoning model across open- and closed-weight models. ToolUniverse standardizes how AI scientists identify and call tools by providing more than 600 machine learning models, datasets, APIs, and scientific packages for data analysis, knowledge retrieval, and experimental design

contributions.statement: A unified AI-tool interaction protocol — comprising a specification schema, interaction schema, and communication methods — standardizes how AI scientist systems issue tool requests and receive results across heterogeneous local and remote tools.
contributions.quote: analogous to the role of HTTP in regulating internet communication, AI-tool interaction protocol in ToolUniverse governs how AI scientists issue tool requests and receive results of executing the tools. The protocol implements two operations: Find Tool, which maps natural-language descriptions of tools to tool specifications … and Call Tool, which executes a selected tool with arguments and returns the result.
contributions.quote_verbatim: analogous to the role of HTTP in regulating internet communication (Figure 1 c), AI-tool interaction protocol in ToolUniverse governs how AI scientists issue tool requests and receive results of executing the tools. The protocol implements two operations (Figure 1 d) in ToolUniverse : Find Tool , which maps natural-language descriptions of tools to tool specifications that are understood by the underlying AI

contributions.statement: Tool Discoverer automatically generates new, executable, and validated scientific tools from natural language descriptions, iteratively refining their specifications for correctness and maintainability.
contributions.quote: Tool Discoverer generates new tools from natural language descriptions. It synthesizes formal specifications, produces executable implementations, validates outputs, and iteratively refines quality. This component ensures that newly created tools are maintainable and can be integrated into ToolUniverse.
contributions.quote_verbatim: Tool Discoverer generates new tools from natural language descriptions. It synthesizes formal specifications, produces executable implementations, validates outputs, and iteratively refines quality. This component ensures that newly created tools are maintainable and can be integrated into ToolUniverse

contributions.statement: Tool Optimizer improves existing tool specifications through iterative test-case generation, execution analysis, and feedback-driven refinement, increasing composability and reducing redundancy across tool descriptions.
contributions.quote: The tool optimizer improves existing tool specifications through iterative refinement. By generating test cases, analyzing executions, and applying feedback from the analyzer agentic tool, it increases usability and removes redundancy in the descriptions of the tool specifications.
contributions.quote_verbatim: The tool optimizer improves existing tool specifications through iterative refinement. By generating test cases, analyzing executions, and applying feedback from the analyzer agentic tool, it increases usability and removes redundancy in the descriptions of the tool specifications.

contributions.statement: Connecting ToolUniverse to an agentic AI model enables end-to-end therapeutic discovery; in a hypercholesterolemia case study this approach identified a potent drug analog with favorable predicted properties.
contributions.quote: In a case study of hypercholesterolemia, ToolUniverse was used to create an AI scientist to identify a potent analog of a drug with favorable predicted properties.
contributions.quote_verbatim: In a case study of hypercholesterolemia, ToolUniverse was used to create an AI scientist to identify a potent analog of a drug with favorable predicted properties.

contributions.statement: LAB-Bench — a dataset of over 2,400 multiple-choice questions spanning literature recall and reasoning, figure and table interpretation, biological database access, protocol troubleshooting, and DNA/protein sequence comprehension and manipulation — provides a benchmark for evaluating AI systems on practical biology research tasks rather than textbook-style science knowledge.
contributions.quote: we introduce the Language Agent Biology Benchmark (LAB-Bench), a broad dataset of over 2,400 multiple choice questions for evaluating AI systems on a range of practical biology research capabilities, including recall and reasoning over literature, interpretation of figures, access and navigation of databases, and comprehension and manipulation of DNA and protein sequences
contributions.quote_verbatim: e introduce the Language Agent Biology Benchmark ( LAB-Bench ), a broad dataset of over 2,400 multiple choice questions for evaluating AI systems on a range of practical biology research capabilities, including recall and reasoning over literature, interpretation of figures, access and navigation of databases, and comprehension and manipulation of DNA and protein sequence

contributions.statement: Cloning Scenarios — a set of 41 multi-step, 'human-hard' multiple-choice questions reflecting real-world molecular cloning workflows — constitute a benchmark whose consistent high-accuracy solution may be both a necessary and sufficient indicator of AI system utility as a scientific collaborator for molecular biologists.
contributions.quote: We also introduce a set of 41 Cloning Scenarios, which are 'human-hard' multi-step multiple-choice questions that we expect may take a trained molecular biologist more than 10 minutes to answer completely. We believe that achieving high accuracy on the Cloning Scenarios is likely a sufficient condition for an AI system to add value as a scientific collaborator or assistant for human molecular biologists.
contributions.quote_verbatim: We also introduce a set of 41 Cloning Scenarios, which are “human-hard” multi-step multiple-choice questions that we expect may take a trained molecular biologist more than 10 minutes to answer completely. We believe that achieving high accuracy on the Cloning Scenarios is likely a sufficient condition for an AI system to add value as a scientific collaborator or assistant for human molecular biologists.

contributions.statement: Systematic evaluation of multiple frontier commercial and open-source language models on practical biology research tasks, with comparison against PhD-level biology researcher performance, reveals the current capabilities and gaps of frontier LLMs relative to human experts on real-world scientific workflows.
contributions.quote: we assess the performance of several frontier commercial and open-source models against LAB-Bench, and further compare model performance against a group of PhD-level biology researchers to assess their capabilities relative to expert humans
contributions.quote_verbatim: e assess the performance of several frontier commercial and open-source models against LAB-Bench , and further compare model performance against a group of PhD-level biology researchers to assess their capabilities relative to expert humans

contributions.statement: A question-generation strategy in which frontier models propose candidate questions, nonsensical or unanswerable items are identified and discarded, and remaining items are manually revised, reliably produces high-quality evaluation questions that exploit the model's reasoning 'null space' and resist easy solution by the generating model.
contributions.quote: we identified a strategy for generating candidate questions for certain subtasks that is likely extensible to other evals, whereby we ask frontier models to generate questions, identify nonsensical questions or those that have no correct answers among the multiple choice options provided by the model, and manually revise those questions. This approach often provides high-quality questions that models cannot readily answer.
contributions.quote_verbatim: We identified a strategy for generating candidate questions for certain subtasks that is likely extensible to other evals, whereby we ask frontier models to generate questions, identify nonsensical questions or those that have no correct answers among the multiple choice options provided by the model, and manually revise those questions. This approach often provides high-quality questions that models cannot readily answer.

contributions.statement: High-quality answer distractors are essential for accurate multiple-choice benchmark assessment: models achieve inflated scores on weak-distractor questions through elimination heuristics despite poor underlying reasoning, making open-answer validation a necessary complement to multiple-choice evaluation.
contributions.quote: High-quality distractors are essential to accurate performance assessment. On particularly hard questions, models appear to achieve high benchmark performance despite poor reasoning abilities by leveraging good test-taking practice (e.g. eliminating unreasonable distractors and guessing). This points to the necessity of validating results on multiple choice questions by manual grading of the same questions in an open-answer format.
contributions.quote_verbatim: High-quality distractors are essential to accurate performance assessment. On particularly hard questions, models appear to achieve high benchmark performance despite poor reasoning abilities by leveraging good test-taking practice (e.g. eliminating unreasonable distractors and guessing). This points to the necessity of validating results on multiple choice questions by manual grading of the same questions in an open-answer format.

contributions.statement: MMReview — a multidisciplinary, multimodal benchmark for automated academic peer review comprising 240 curated papers with expert-written reviews across 17 research domains in four disciplines (Artificial Intelligence, Natural Sciences, Engineering Sciences, Social Sciences), incorporating three input modalities: manuscript text, embedded figures and tables, and rendered PDF page images.
contributions.quote: We introduce MMReview, the first comprehensive evaluation benchmark for automated academic peer review using LLMs, spanning multiple disciplines and modalities. Built upon our data filtering and generation pipeline, MMReview comprises 240 high-quality samples across 17 academic fields in 4 disciplines.
contributions.quote_verbatim: We introduce MMReview , the first comprehensive evaluation benchmark for automated academic peer review using LLMs, spanning multiple disciplines and modalities. Built upon our data filtering and generation pipeline, MMReview comprises 240 high-quality samples across 17 academic fields in 4 disciplines

contributions.statement: A suite of 13 peer-review evaluation tasks (6,724 questions) grouped into four categories — step-wise review generation, outcome formulation, human preference alignment, and adversarial robustness — enabling multi-dimensional assessment of LLM and MLLM capabilities in automated peer review.
contributions.quote: We meticulously design 13 distinct tasks encompassing a total of 6,724 thoughtfully curated questions, enabling multi-dimensional evaluation of model performance. These diverse tasks allow for targeted assessment and facilitate the identification of potential limitations in LLM-generated peer review content.
contributions.quote_verbatim: We meticulously design 13 distinct tasks encompassing a total of 6,724 thoughtfully curated questions, enabling multi-dimensional evaluation of model performance. These diverse tasks allow for targeted assessment and facilitate the identification of potential limitations in LLM-generated peer review content.

contributions.statement: Empirical findings from evaluating 21 LLMs (18 open-source, 3 closed-source) on automated peer review tasks reveal key capability gaps and domain-specific performance patterns that inform future development of LLM-assisted review systems.
contributions.quote: We conduct extensive experiments on 18 open-source and 3 closed-source models using the MMReview benchmark, offering some key findings of LLM-based automated reviewing. Our findings offer in-depth analysis and valuable guidance for the future development of LLM-assisted peer review systems.
contributions.quote_verbatim: We conduct extensive experiments on 18 open-source and 3 closed-source models using the MMReview benchmark, offering some key findings of LLM-based automated reviewing. Our findings offer in-depth analysis and valuable guidance for the future development of LLM-assisted peer review systems.

contributions.statement: SciMuse — a system that combines a knowledge graph built from 58 million scientific papers with GPT-4 to generate personalized, interdisciplinary research collaboration ideas for individual scientists.
contributions.quote: Here, we introduce SciMuse, which uses 58 million research papers and a large-language model to generate research ideas.
contributions.quote_verbatim: Here, we introduce SciMuse , which uses 58 million research papers and a large-language model to generate research ideas

contributions.statement: A large-scale human evaluation dataset of over 4,400 AI-generated research ideas rated by more than 100 research group leaders spanning natural sciences, social sciences, and humanities, enabling empirical assessment of what makes AI-generated research ideas compelling to experienced scientists.
contributions.quote: We conduct a large-scale evaluation in which over 100 research group leaders -- from natural sciences to humanities -- ranked more than 4,400 personalized ideas based on their interest.
contributions.quote_verbatim: We conduct a large-scale evaluation in which over 100 research group leaders – from natural sciences to humanities – ranked more than 4,400 personalized ideas based on their interest. T

contributions.statement: Supervised neural networks trained on expert human evaluations can predict the interest level of AI-generated research ideas from knowledge graph features, enabling quality filtering without additional human annotation.
contributions.quote: This data allows us to predict research interest using (1) supervised neural networks trained on human evaluations
contributions.quote_verbatim: This data allows us to predict research interest using (1) supervised neural networks trained on human evaluations

contributions.statement: Zero-shot LLM-based ranking of AI-generated research suggestions, without access to human evaluation data, can predict expert interest levels, providing a scalable alternative when expert-labeled data is unavailable.
contributions.quote: (2) unsupervised zero-shot ranking with large-language models. Our results demonstrate how future systems can help generating compelling research ideas and foster unforeseen interdisciplinary collaborations.
contributions.quote_verbatim: (2) unsupervised zero-shot ranking with large-language models. Our results demonstrate how future systems can help generating compelling research ideas and foster unforeseen interdisciplinary collaborations.

contributions.statement: A large scientific knowledge graph of 123,128 concepts and their co-occurrence relationships derived from 58 million papers (OpenAlex), augmented with citation impact signals, supports personalized subgraph extraction for individual researchers.
contributions.quote: The knowledge graph, depicted in Fig. 1 (a), consists of vertices, representing scientific concepts, and edges are drawn when two concepts jointly appear in a title or abstract of a scientific paper... resulting in 123,128 concepts in the natural and social sciences. We then use more than 58 million scientific papers from the open-source database OpenAlex to create edges.
contributions.quote_verbatim: The knowledge graph, depicted in Fig. 1 (a), consists of vertices, representing scientific concepts, and edges are drawn when two concepts jointly appear in a title or abstract of a scientific paper. The concept list is generated from the titles and abstracts of approximately 2.44 million papers from arXiv, bioRxiv, ChemRxiv, and medRxiv, with a data cutoff in February 2

contributions.statement: VIRSCI — a multi-agent LLM system that organizes virtual scientists (cloned from real researcher profiles and publications) into teams to collaboratively generate, evaluate, and refine scientific research ideas through five structured stages: collaborator selection, topic discussion, idea generation, novelty assessment, and abstract generation.
contributions.quote: we propose the first multi-agent system with a scientific research ecosystem for conducting and benchmarking scientific collaborations, named VIRSCI, where real data is used for role-playing and objective evaluation.

contributions.statement: An end-to-end inter- and intra-team discussion mechanism for multi-agent scientific idea generation, where agents within a team iteratively discuss while also proactively soliciting advice from external agents via an 'Invitation Mechanism', improving communication topology and simulation realism.
contributions.quote: we propose an end-to-end pipeline that spans team organization to idea generation. A novel inter- and intra-team discussion mechanism is introduced to promote communication topology and enhance the simulation realism.

contributions.statement: A virtual Scientific Research Ecosystem — a digital twin of a research community at a fixed point in time — comprising virtual scientists whose profiles mirror real researchers, a past-paper database, and a contemporary-paper database, enabling grounded benchmarking of multi-agent scientific collaboration.
contributions.quote: we build a virtual Scientific Research Ecosystem to benchmark the potential of multi-agent systems in scientific idea generation. Specifically, Scientific Research Ecosystem is a digit twin of research communities at the given time point

contributions.quote: The findings prove that the multi-agent system improves the single-agent executive by, on average, +13.8% and +44.1% in alignment with and potential impacts on contemporary research, respectively. Furthermore, our experiments investigate collaboration mechanisms among agents that influence the performance of idea generation. The patterns observed in the experimental results align with findings from prior Science of Science studies

contributions.statement: Chain-of-Ideas (CoI) agent — an LLM-based research ideation system that organizes relevant literature into a chronological chain structure mirroring the progressive development of a research domain, enabling LLMs to generate novel and logically coherent research ideas with experimental designs for approximately $0.50 per idea.
contributions.quote: We propose CoI agent to enhance LLMs' capability in idea generation. CoI agent effectively mirrors the progressive nature of research development, allowing LLMs to better grasp the current research advancements.

contributions.statement: Idea Arena — an arena-style, pairwise evaluation protocol for comparing research idea-generation methods that achieves high agreement with human researcher preferences.
contributions.quote: We propose Idea Arena for a comprehensive evaluation of idea-generation methods, which shows high agreement with human researchers.
contributions.quote_verbatim:
We propose Idea Arena
for a comprehensive evaluation of idea-generation
methods, which shows high agreement with human
researchers.

contributions.statement: Organizing literature by developmental trend rather than by quantity of related papers is more pivotal for LLM-generated idea novelty.
contributions.quote: Our analysis further shows that for LLMs to generate novel ideas, a clear developmental trend analysis is more pivotal than the quantity of related literature.
contributions.quote_verbatim:
Our
analysis further shows that for LLMs to generate

contributions.statement: A multi-domain corpus of 1,250 scientific papers with annotated future research ideas (FRIs) spanning Computer Science, Economics, Chemistry, Physics, and Medicine, designed to benchmark LLM future-research-idea generation with author-proposed ideas as ground truth.
contributions.quote: we create a novel dataset of recent papers of five domains (Computer science, Economics, Chemistry, Physics, Medical)

contributions.statement: Idea Alignment Score (IAScore) — an automated, scalable metric that measures how well LLM-generated research ideas align with the future directions proposed by the paper's own authors, serving as a lower-bound indicator of a model's idea-generation competence.
contributions.quote: we propose an Idea Alignment Score (IAScore). This score reflects how well the generated ideas align with those proposed by the authors. Our proposed IAScore provides a scalable, automated, and interpretable metric that captures how well a model can generate author-aligned research directions.

contributions.statement: Idea Distinctness Index — an automated metric for quantifying the diversity of research ideas generated by LLMs, complementing alignment-based evaluation with a measure of non-redundancy.
contributions.quote: To assess the models' ability to generate diverse ideas, we introduce the Idea Distinctness Index.
contributions.quote_verbatim:
To assess the models’ ability
to generate diverse ideas, we introduce the Idea
Distinctness Index.

contributions.statement: LLMs (Gemini, Claude-2, GPT-3.5, GPT-4) can generate future research ideas that are relevant, feasible, and novel to a meaningful degree, as evidenced by human evaluation of 660 generated ideas across novelty, relevance, and feasibility dimensions.
contributions.quote: We further conduct a human evaluation of 660 generated ideas in the field of computer science to assess their novelty, relevance, and feasibility… Our findings demonstrate that LLMs have the potential to generate research ideas that are relevant, distinct, feasible, and novel—to some extent.

contributions.statement: NOVA — an iterative planning framework that purposely plans the retrieval of external knowledge across multiple fields at each step, progressively enriching LLM-based research-idea generation with broader and deeper insights to improve novelty and diversity.
contributions.quote: we introduce an iterative planning framework for LLM-based idea generation that specifically targets the enhancement of the novelty and diversity of the ideas produced. Starting with seed ideas that are generated using scientific innovation theories and related knowledge, our framework undergoes multiple iterations of planning and searching.

contributions.statement: A multi-source seed idea generation module that primes LLM idea generation by conditioning on established scientific innovation theories (e.g., Kuhn, Popper, Lakatos), recent research trends, and domain expertise, along with explicit reasoning traces for each proposed idea.
contributions.quote: we propose a multi-source seed idea generation module. Upon receiving an input paper, the module activates the LLM to generate initial seed ideas, drawing on scientific innovation theories, recent research trends, and related works.

contributions.statement: Integrating planning methodologies into LLM-based scientific research tasks — using goal-oriented search plans to direct knowledge retrieval — produces 3.4× more unique novel ideas compared to unplanned retrieval and at least 2.5× more top-rated ideas than state-of-the-art baselines across 170 seed papers in a Swiss Tournament evaluation.
contributions.quote: The number of unique novel ideas produced by our framework is 3.4 times higher than without it. Moreover, our method outperforms the current state-of-the-art, generating at least 2.5 times more top-rated ideas based on 170 seed papers in a Swiss Tournament evaluation.
